Fees are per transaction. Sum of inputs minus sum of outputs is the fee.

Using different addresses per transaction is not anonymization. Maybe you can "mix your own coins", but I think using a real mixer (or two) is better.

You can't merge your coins without paying a fee unless you have a nice miner and zero transactions in mempool. If anything, more complicated transactions require higher fees. Fees are more often looked at as fee per byte.

Now, if you send from 10 wallets into 1 wallet (in a single transaction) you will pay less TX fees up front and when you want to spend from that wallet since it'll be a single output vs multiple. So there is value in what you want to do.

Good news: `pook` is now able to work with multiple mock engines that can be defined the user programmatically, as I described in the proposal above.

I think this provides strong freedom and versatility to package consumers in case that they want to use a custom HTTP mock engine, such as in your case with `mocket`.

For implementation details, please see: https://github.com/h2non/pook/commit/1ea37962a741fa23dadbf9d7c933f8484d640f89

Mock engine specific API documentation:
http://pook.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pook.MockEngine

This feature was introduced in `pook@0.1.7` and it's available via `PyPI` registry:
https://github.com/h2non/pook/blob/master/History.rst#v017--2016-12-18

If you have the chance to build an adapter for `mocket`, which I think should not be hard, please feel free to share your thoughts here.

If it works consistently, I'm open to add specific support for `mocket` as optional, activable capability in `pook` by default, also adding explicit support notes across documentation.

I wrote up a test yesterday that found similar issues and I've written fixes for it (nested structures and handling for lists.) I also noticed an odd edge case that sometimes the replacements would occasionally sub in Python datatypes and stringify it (e.g. `"{u'key': u'val'}"`) so I added support for that as well. 

With these changes, however, your first test of `coerce.yaml` points out something interesting. If we recursively coerce a structure, then value types that are stringy but have a datatype inherent in them are going to be parsed into their valid types: in particular `one_string` is going to become an integer and not a string.

Another thing I came up against was that stringy datatypes passed as environment variables might be too tricky at this stage to properly parse. Consider the JSON string `{"inner_key": "val"`; after substitution as a value to a key, this will cause problems for parsers as the conflicting double quotes: `{"key": "{"inner_key": "val"}"}`. At the moment I'm simply assuming that environment variables are purely for primitive types. Theoretically, this is something that could be implemented later instead of in this PR. 

I'll push my changes but I'll need to think some more about how to approach fixing some of the quirks.

I guess unittest's autodiscover is loading examples/clientserver/client.py looking for tests and in that process the code gets executed (as the module lacks an `if __name__=='main'` condition). client.py is an example module that needs the example module server.py to also be running, that's why it fails in your case.

As they are now, pydbus tests are not meant to be discovered (although [that may change in future releases](https://github.com/LEW21/pydbus/pull/30)). 

pydbus' tests need to be run in a context where a session DBUS daemon is running. I you run the tests inside your desktop session, it will probably already be one, but that won't be the case if, for example, your package is tested in a build server, a chroot environment or a minimal docker container. tests/run.sh launches such daemon and runs the tests (also have a look at some improvements I proposed in https://github.com/LEW21/pydbus/pull/38). I guess your best bet would be using that script or some variation of it in the test phase of your package build.

I'm in the process of packaging pydbus for Debian, and have used [such a script](https://anonscm.debian.org/cgit/python-modules/packages/pydbus.git/tree/debian/run_tests.sh) to run the tests.

I don't think it's worth the effort to add a lock inside Kernel.run, or
that it would be very helpful to users. Most objects in Python are unsafe
to simultaneously call methods on from different threads, unless they
explicitly say otherwise. The convention is that if you want to start
messing about with an object from multiple threads, then it's your
responsibility to come up with some locking strategy.

Forgive the drive-by comment, but preserving the existing rules is nice for legacy code. Though, to confound the issue, I'll add the parsing behavior from GNU units which I've always found fairly natural. It treats all implicit multiplication as higher-precedence:

    $ units '600mg/(24hours)'
        Definition: 6.9444444e-09 kg / s
    $ units '600mg/24hours'
        Definition: 6.9444444e-09 kg / s
    $ units '600mg/24 hours'
        Definition: 6.9444444e-09 kg / s
    $ units '600mg/24*hours'
        Definition: 0.09 kg s
    $ units '600 mg/24hr'
        Definition: 6.9444444e-09 kg / s

It is not a bug of IPython nor a bug in Pint. It is just how the IPython widget's work. When you do say `num=2`, IPython detects that is a number and creates a range of possible values. You could achieve something like this with a Selection Widget (ie. you specify the a list of quantities yourself) or with:

```python
from IPython.html.widgets.interaction import interact
import pint
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()

@interact
def square(num=2):
    num = num * ureg.meter
    q = 2 * num
    print("{} times 2 is {}".format(num, num*2))
```

> What about adding a switch that tells cget to git clone from github

No not directly, as cloning is much slower and when cloning recursively there isn't a simple way to shallow clone the submodules. I will probably add a way to add git urls(like `git://github.com/brunocodutra/metal.git`) in the future.

> instead of downloading their stupid tarballs that don't bundle submodules?

An open-source project should provide source tarballs for release, it doesn't necessarily need to be from github.

Furthermore, submodules are usually used as a poor-dependency management, which requires the user to download the dependency multiple times when used in multiple projects. And this is the case when the user installs both metal and alloy. Instead, the cmake-utils should be made installable and found by `find_package(cmake-utils)`(that is how [BCM](https://github.com/boost-cmake/bcm) works). When its not found, it can fallback on the submodule. This way cget can install the cmake-utils once, and it can be used both by metal and alloy.

The problem lies in this: the `Minimizer.initial_guesses` property returns the initial guesses for the non-fixed parameters. The objective has been `partial`ed to expect only those arguments, the fixed ones have been provided by partial:
```python
self.objective = partial(objective, **{p.name: p.value for p in self._fixed_params})
```
However, the constraints have never been adapted to this, so they are still `len(self._fixed_params)` short. Therefore, the solution is to also `partial` the constraints. However, `scipy_constraints` is a `staticmethod` so it is agnostic about which parameters are fixed. Some thought is needed.

No, the annotated contigs will always be the original contigs. However, the indexes of the scaffolds will change from pass to pass -- scaffolds between passes should not be compared. 

For instance, say that the first pass generated two scaffolds, 1 and 2, where scaffold 1 contains contigs 1,2,3 and scaffold2 contains  4 and 5. The next pass might scaffold the two scaffolds 1 and 2 into one, with index 1 (index are independent from pass to pass). In pass two, scaffold1 will consist of contigs 1,2,3,4,5 and they will be annotated to scaffold1 in `info-pass2.agp`.

Your example tells me that `scaffold_1` contains contig `PE150bp.genome2_contig-1284000033` in position 0 to 4789 and the contig is reverse complemented (w.r.t. the scaffold in the fasta file). This means you have to search for the reverse compement of the contig if you want to find it in the scaffold.

Hi,
when binding you must provide a full context, so you cannot provide a "key" to search for in multiple organization.

If you're sure that your names are unique troughtout your LDAP tree you have thre different approach for accomplish what you need:

1. Perform a search (anonymous or with a user that can read all other user names) on the whole tree for the naming attribute and then perform a bind with the dn you've found
2. Perform a search with a user that can read all the other user names and can make a Compare operation on the userPassword attribute) and then perform a compare operation for the attribute userPassword in the dn you've found. (Not sure if this works for Active Directory)
3. If your tree is not deep nested you can try the bind operation with the user and password in all the ou where users are defined.

I'd suggest the first option, but this means that you have to include the admin password (or an equivalent) in your code in case your LDAP server doesn't returns users in an anonymous search.

Let me know if you need some examples.

```python
            target_name = gssapi.Name('ldap@' + hostname, gssapi.NameType.hostbased_service)
        else:

    else:

    creds = gssapi.Credentials(name=gssapi.Name(connection.user), usage='initiate') if connection.user else None
```

Txdbus was designed from scratch to provide a good async interface to the
DBus protocol. I made no effort to mimic the python-dbus interface so there
are bound to be wide divergences in how the two interfaces are used. Just
looking at the API may be a bit misleading though and the real question is
whether or not txdbus is missing functionality. What can you do with
python-dbus that is either inconvenient or unachievable with txdbus?

> method(dbus_interface, in_signature=None, out_signature=None,

> source code
> Factory for decorators used to mark methods of a dbus.service.Object to be
> exported on the D-Bus.
>
> The decorated method will be exported over D-Bus as the method of the same
> name on the given D-Bus interface.
>
> Parameters:
> dbus_interface (str) - Name of a D-Bus interface
> in_signature (str or None) - If not None, the signature of the method
> parameters in the usual D-Bus notation
> out_signature (str or None) - If not None, the signature of the return
> value in the usual D-Bus notation
> async_callbacks (tuple containing (str,str), or None) - If None (default)
> the decorated method is expected to return values matching the
> out_signature as usual, or raise an exception on error. If not None, the
> following applies:
>
> async_callbacks contains the names of two keyword arguments to the
> decorated function, which will be used to provide a success callback and an
> error callback (in that order).
>
> When the decorated method is called via the D-Bus, its normal return value
> will be ignored; instead, a pair of callbacks are passed as keyword
> arguments, and the decorated method is expected to arrange for one of them
> to be called.
>
> On success the success callback must be called, passing the results of
> this method as positional parameters in the format given by the
> out_signature.
>
> On error the decorated method may either raise an exception before it
> returns, or arrange for the error callback to be called with an Exception
> instance as parameter.
>

We use yas3fs to archive files by taring them up onto the mounted filesystem and if that succeeds we remove the files.  With the current behavior data loss is very likely if we lose write access to the bucket or never had it).  I think there are a few opportunities for improvement.  1) Mark files in the cache (perhaps use a different directory in the cache) for pending writes then move them to the read cache after successfully written.  Then try to upload them at some point in the future, at least on subsequent mount.  2) If all writes are failing stop accepting writes by returning I/O error or permission denied to the filesystem write.

Jason,

Thanks for the response.  I'm going to share two assumptions I have which is driving my perspective on this issue:

1. Anyone going through the extra work of using the keyring library wants a more secure implementation than plaintext, otherwise they would likely have just used a config file.
2. Many/most developers don't read documentation and will use default settings unless the program flat-out doesn't work, at which point they will read more closely.

I realize that these assumptions may not always be valid, but IMO we should approach the solution to this issue assuming the above is true and then allowing devs to make exceptions as needed.

> Although the use of the priority < 1 seems arbitrary, the measure has a documented purpose. A priority of < 1, > 0 is suitable but a priority >= 1 is recommended. I believe that's the best indicator keyring can give about backends without more detail about the user's environment and usage.

I understand that there is some ambiguity here based on a user's use-case.  I guess, I would argue, that the plain-text option is really "unsuitable" in most instances and sometimes suitable.  Maybe the terms should be updated to "available" and "recommended."

> One would be to move the Plaintext keyring out of the project altogether and require it to be included explicitly as a separate package if the user desires for it to be available.

Seems like too much work to me.  :)

> A less invasive approach could be to disable the Plaintext Keyring by default, and only enable it by API or environment variable (opt-in)

I like this.  Although, I'd like to adjust the proposal to make anything less than recommended opt-in.  That way, the default implementation is most secure and there is extra effort required on the devs part to make less secure.  Requiring extra effort will also likely lead to the dev reading the docs more closely and understanding better the tradeoffs for what they are choosing to do.

I propose a api variable something like:

```
#!python

keyring.allow_unrecommended_backends = True

```

or, if you don't like the connotation of "unrecommended", then maybe:

```
#!python

keyring.allow_less_secure_backends = True

```

Either one will, IMO, encourage devs to read the docs more carefully and understand the tradeoffs of which backend they are using.

If no recommended backends are available, and the API setting isn't enabled, then I would prefer to see an exception thrown indicating such.

I realize this would lead to some backwards incompatibility issues.  IMO, that is acceptable.  In order to ease the transition, I would encourage you to make a an initial release that issues a deprecation warning if an unrecommended backend is used by default without the explicit opt-in.  Then, make a new major release 3-6 months later which implements the API change.

> Another approach could involve raising a UserWarning when passwords are saved in the plaintext keyring (and implementers could suppress that warning in the API or with an environment variable).

This is better than nothing, but I like the above option better.  If you are going to require the dev to make changes anyway, then lets just make the implementation opt-in.  Most secure by default, less secure if they choose.

> The latter two options might prove inconvenient on systems where security of the file is not a concern.

Based on my first assumption, this is going to be the less-common case.  Therefore, I'm proposing that we accept the inconvenience in favor of a default implementation that is most secure.

> I'm eager to hear your responses on these proposals. I still agree that it's important to address this issue and find a less surprising outcome in this case.

Thanks for inviting feedback.  I hope my thoughts are helpful.

Your patch is now outdated, and we won't accept it" would have been much, much better.

2) the patch attempts to doo to much -> maybe a really small bit. Maybe it would have been good to split the _keyring_backend modification and the better import. But at the time the project was quite messy and the small modification of adding a _keyring_backend (which was a good idea, now a similar result is achieved in init_backend(), I see) at the beginning in a single commit would do little harm. The other part is quite a lot of code, but from a semantic POV it's a single change.

3) Sure, it may be deprecated. By the way python 2.7 with importlib was just a few months old when I released this patch, and at the time I thought that it was a bit limiting to prevent python < 2.7 to work with keyring, or to require an external library for a single functionality. In hindsight, today importlib would be a good choice.

4) There's no comment but the code is clear. The original code made little sense. There's no way ConfigParser could raise the error - the original code checks for such an option in a safe way ( config.get ... ) then raises NoOptionError and catches it later. Since the patch proposal makes the code much shorter, such internal try/catching makes no sense and it's just a matter of if/else. Behaviour is just the same.

5) That's true; I should probably have deleted that as well, since my idea was to just let the keyring be loaded from its FQDN, so it should be in sys.path elsewere. I still think that's a better way to go (manual fiddling with load path is a bad idea, IMHO)

6) "NoneType" has no attribute "something" tells much more than a stray NameError. I couldn't fix everything with a single patch. A more complex proposal I submitted years ago to the keyring ML (no answer, of course) said that the current keyring implementation sucks, and we should create a keyring object with different implementations which could then be used by client programs, instead of relying on free functions that rely on global configuration.

I took my time to discuss keyring design and I had little response:

Thanks @buzzrick. Generally speaking, it is safe to share the RSA public key publicly (hence the name) as long as you don't share the RSA private key. I've had a chance to look over what you sent me and I think I've figured out where the issue lies.

PyJWT's documentation talks about accepting two kinds of RSA public keys: PEM and SSH format. PEM keys start with "-----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----" and SSH format keys start with "ssh-rsa". The file you sent me starts with "-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----" indicating that it is a x509 certificate in PEM format. A certificate is actually a public key accompanied by certain statements about that key (who owns it, how long it is valid, etc.) that are together digitally signed by a third-party authority who is vouching for its authenticity. However, an x509 certificate is not exactly an RSA key in PEM format... even though it does actually contain the RSA public key. That is why you are getting an error.

I was curious so I went back to v0.3.2 (cd32cf25) and ran the following:

```
>>> cert = open('certificate.cer', 'r').read()
>>> import jwt
>>> jwt.prepare_RS_key(cert)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "jwt/__init__.py", line 105, in prepare_RS_key
    key = RSA.importKey(key)
  File "/home/mark/.venv/pyjwt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Crypto/PublicKey/RSA.py", line 665, in importKey
    return self._importKeyDER(der)
  File "/home/mark/.venv/pyjwt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Crypto/PublicKey/RSA.py", line 588, in _importKeyDER
    raise ValueError("RSA key format is not supported")
ValueError: RSA key format is not supported
```

It seems like the v0.3.2 also rejects x509 certificates so I'm not sure how it was working before unless something changed with regards to how you were generating your keys. At any point in time did you change from generating PEM public keys to generating PEM x509 certificates?

The good news is... there are some easy workarounds to solve this problem since the RSA public key can be extracted from the x509 certificate.

First, OpenSSL can extract the key for you in PEM format by running the following command: 
```
openssl x509 -in certificate.cer -noout -pubkey > public_key.pem
```

You can then read public_key.pem in and use it as the key in PyJWT.

Second, if you like doing things in Python, cryptography can help you extract the public key from the certificate:
```
from cryptography.x509 import load_pem_x509_certificate
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend

certificate_text = open('certificate.cer', 'r').read()
certificate = load_pem_x509_certificate(certificate_text, default_backend())
publickey = certificate.public_key()
```

The publickey variable can then be passed in as the key to `verify()`.

I hope that helps! I'm sorry I can't help explain why you didn't seem to run into this problem with v0.3.2 but it appears that the older version couldn't import x509 certificates either.

Also, one last recommendation: most people consider 1024-bit RSA keys to be risky now as computers have gotten better and better at being able to brute-force them. Generally, most people have begun transitioning to 2048-bit (or higher) keys. Just something to consider. :-)

> Well, the definition, which timezones have which offset, is pretty fixed, isn't it? 

That's what I thought too but it turns out it's more complicated than that :) The Olson tz database changes quite frequently: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database The main reason seems to be updates in daylight savings time. I'm not looking forward to having to keep up with that as a maintainer :)

The alternative that I'd prefer is that we add an optional argument representing the timezone or the tz offset – instead of passing a human readable identifier (== the string tz abbreviation).

Then people could just use [pytz](http://pytz.sourceforge.net/) or whatever other tz database they'd like to use to lookup the offset – alleviating schedule from the maintenance burden.

```python
from pytz import timezone

EST = timezone('US/Eastern')
schedule.every().day.at("10:00", EST)

schedule.every().day.at("10:00", pytz.utc)
```

> I think there definitely needs to be a kwarg on the TinyDB class for changing the Element during the database initialisation.

Seems like we all agree on this change. Would you feel comforable with setting up a Pull Request?

> I also think it is desirable to get rid of the '2 step' element creation (dict > element). Unfortunately you can't pass Element to the json parser via object_hook, because nested dicts would also look like elements.

The downside to this is that it only works with the JSONStorage. If we'd chose that route, then every Storage which doesn't have a mechanism similar to `object_hook` (e.g. the MemoryStorage we provide) would have to implement it by itself which in the end would result in more or less the same iteration based approach we already have.

> An alternative solution to the '2 step' problem might be for Element to be a proxy to the dict returned by the json parser. This way the creation of each element instance would be far 'lighter'. This could look like the following (ignoring the immutable eid property for now);

I don't see how that's different from what we already do as the `Element` class is a dict proxy with only adding the `eid` attribute. Or did I misunderstand you here?

> I actually think StorageProxy can be removed entirely, and the functionallity moved into the Table class. Currently, tables don't actually know their own name! So Table.__init__() could accept and use the name arg, just like StorageProxy does. Additionally, Table._read() would effectively be replaced with StorageProxy.read(), and the table would handle creating elements (the table would also pass itself to the element at this point). This could then be leveraged to implement the lazy creation of Elements previously discussed.

Well, the reason for the existence of StorageProxy is that I first started out with the TinyDB class and then later added Tables which used the TinyDB `_read`/`_write` methods to get access to the storage. But this required the tables to always keep a reference to the master TinyDB instance. To solve this commit b79ce35addbdcd2d368e710d7149d009eafd7834 introduced the StorageProxy class which is created by the TinyDB instance and passed to the table. The StorageProxy then is responsible for extracting the table contents from the database dict and writing the data back to the right place. What I like about this approach is that it makes it hard to mess with tables in a way that would result in data being written to a wrong place.

> I tried some sleep yesterday and couldn't get much done, it wouldn't be too reliable either. Is there a way to trigger it on release?

No, there's no support for release triggers. There hasn't been much need yet, and you can always just process events individually, which is what `add_hotkey` does behind the curtains. For example:

```
def process(event):
    if event.event_type == 'down': return
    if (event.name == 'c' and keyboard.is_pressed('win')) or (event.name == 'win' and keyboard.is_pressed('c')):
        print('triggered')
keyboard.hook(process)
```

> Another question, whenever I hit win+c, the hotkey is called but the letter c is also printed, is there a way to stop this? I think it's a similar function to stopPropagation() from javascript.

Yes, but in Windows only at the moment. I just merged a pull request from @xoviat that deals with this. For Linux suppressing keys requires cooperating from X, which the library avoids at the moment. But this is an important feature that is on the roadmap.

> P.S.: Sorry for a newbie Python question, but what would be the correct syntax for setting the delay here?

You almost got it, just pass the `1 second` to the inner call instead:

    keyboard.add_hotkey('win+c', lambda: keyboard.call_later(keyboard.send, ['ctrl+a'], 1))

The ofxtools parser could indeed use better error handling.  What would be needed would be the model signaling to the parser whether a violation on any particular field is a deal-breaker or not.

I'd be worried about truncating fields as a matter of policy, because sometimes FIs violate the length restrictions on fields that matter (like investment ticker symbols).

If you look through ofxtools/models.py (search for "relax") you'll see several instances where I changed the spec for exactly this reason... FIs violate the spec because Quicken doesn't enforce it, it's just a string that's too long, and these days everybody has GB of memory, so who really cares?

If it's just STMTTRN.name I don't mind changing it in the source... if you search models.py for "relax" you'll see a few instances where I've already done this, and STMTTRN.name is exactly the kind of field you know FIs are going to abuse.

I'd encourage you to patch your own copy of models.py for the time being - change STMTTRN.name from String(32) to String(255) (line 386) and see if anything else blows up.  Do me a favor and let me know which fields we're having a problem with.

I'm afraid it's not nearly as user friendly as the 2-point code.  It's an executable program - correeeb -- not a python module.  And it doesn't even use parameter files.  All the parameters about the binning and such are in CorrEEEb.cpp, which you have to change by hand and then recompile.  

Plus the documentation is not nearly is good, although there is a Read.me file that explains how to run it.

And finally, it only works in Euclidean coordinates, not RA/Dec, so you'll need to project your positions onto a tangent plane.  (Which the python version of treecorr can do for you, I should point out.)

So if you want to try to use it, you are quite welcome.  However, if you can wait for this issue and #4 to be done (goal for both: Summer 2015), it may be significantly easier to use at that point.

I tried that -- it doesn't actually help.  The problem is that the files in question are encoded in iso-8859-1, not utf.  They are properly marked according to https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0263/ with a first line of #-*- coding: iso-8859-1 -*-

PEP263 gives the instructions for decoding this -- read the first two lines, look for a match for this regexp, etc.

1) We can do that -- it's not rocket science -- but I was wondering if there was an accessible thing in the library to do it already (I think the implementation of PEP263 does it down in the compiler.)

2) Then we have the round-trip issue.  Obviously, we don't need to go back to any random encoding, but I think the default encoding for < Python 3.0 is ASCII, so we probably haven't thought out how to generate non-ASCII characters in the output dump -- we probably need to automatically add the UTF-8 encoding at the top of the file.

This is my durty fix:

```python
def modular_sqrt(a, p):

    def legendre_symbol(a, p):
        """ Compute the Legendre symbol a|p using
            Euler's criterion. p is a prime, a is
            relatively prime to p (if p divides
            a, then a|p = 0)

            Returns 1 if a has a square root modulo
            p, -1 otherwise.
        """
        ls = pow(a, (p - 1) // 2, p)
        return -1 if ls == p - 1 else ls

    """ Find a quadratic residue (mod p) of 'a'. p
        must be an odd prime.

        Solve the congruence of the form:
            x^2 = a (mod p)
        And returns x. Note that p - x is also a root.

        0 is returned is no square root exists for
        these a and p.

        The Tonelli-Shanks algorithm is used (except
        for some simple cases in which the solution
        is known from an identity). This algorithm
        runs in polynomial time (unless the
        generalized Riemann hypothesis is false).
    """
    # Simple cases
    #
    if legendre_symbol(a, p) != 1:
        return 0
    elif a == 0:
        return 0
    elif p == 2:
        return p
    elif p % 4 == 3:
        return pow(a, (p + 1) // 4, p)

    # Partition p-1 to s * 2^e for an odd s (i.e.
    # reduce all the powers of 2 from p-1)
    #
    s = p - 1
    e = 0
    while s % 2 == 0:
        s //= 2
        e += 1

    # Find some 'n' with a legendre symbol n|p = -1.
    # Shouldn't take long.
    #
    n = 2
    while legendre_symbol(n, p) != -1:
        n += 1

    # Here be dragons!
    # Read the paper "Square roots from 1; 24, 51,
    # 10 to Dan Shanks" by Ezra Brown for more
    # information
    #

    # x is a guess of the square root that gets better
    # with each iteration.
    # b is the "fudge factor" - by how much we're off
    # with the guess. The invariant x^2 = ab (mod p)
    # is maintained throughout the loop.
    # g is used for successive powers of n to update
    # both a and b
    # r is the exponent - decreases with each update
    #
    x = pow(a, (s + 1) // 2, p)
    b = pow(a, s, p)
    g = pow(n, s, p)
    r = e

    while True:
        t = b
        m = 0
        for m in range(r):
            if t == 1:
                break
            t = pow(t, 2, p)

        if m == 0:
            return x

        gs = pow(g, 2 ** (r - m - 1), p)
        g = (gs * gs) % p
        x = (x * gs) % p
        b = (b * g) % p
        r = m

print(modular_sqrt(223, 17)) # should return 6
```

You need to go down to the run level and make your text changes there. All character formatting is stored as properties of a run. When you assign to Cell.text you replace all the existing runs with a single new one; that's why your formatting is no longer there after.

This can be tricky because PowerPoint has a habit of making a lot of runs for various reasons.

A place to start might be:

```python
for paragraph in cell.text_frame.paragraphs:
    for run in paragraph:
        print run.text
```

This will give you an idea what you're up against. You could also inspect the XML. If you have control over the original document (like it's a template) you can purposely make the content occupy a single run to make life easier.

There's no API support for this yet. You'd have to figure out what the XML looks like for this and see about manipulating it using `lxml` calls to add these in.

The general idea is to make a simple-as-possible presentation, like one slide, then make a copy of it and add an automation using PowerPoint. Then you can compare the XML of the two and usually pretty quickly get an idea what XML elements/attributes do the job. `opc-diag` is handy for this.

`python-pptx` only requires lxml>=3.1.0, see [requirements.txt here](https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/requirements.txt), so I'm not seeing why pip would trigger an lxml upgrade if you already have 3.6.1 installed.

> I want the module functions to return Command / ConnectedCommand instances.

for what purpose?

an object wrapping the process would be a nice way to separate the process creation from the call that blocks on process completion (allowing multiple creates before any blocking for multiple non-pipelined child processes running in parallel) , but the envoy api never actually supported that -- ``Command.run`` started the process *and* blocked on completion.

it would make it less clumsy to offer the output of both stdout and stderr, but ``Response.std_err`` didn't work; it was never even actually wired up.

it can support getting the stdout of processes other than the one at the tail, but that also requires shuttling all that data through the python parent process blocking on the children one-at-a-time, preventing parallelization of the pipeline.

the API, as it was, was only really good for getting the status code and stdout of the end of the pipeline (the tradeoff for making the other stdouts available isn't remotely worth it). I don't think the API was ever properly thought through, so I tried to make it as simple as it could be without sacrificing worthwhile functionality.

The projects are actually quite similar in features and possibilities so it's mostly a matter of taste :)

Personally I think this project uses a more Pythonic API which makes usage easier and clearer. To illustrate:

This project (as taken from the docs):
```
import statsd
counter = statsd.Counter('MyApplication')
# The actual increment
counter += 1
```
The other project (as taken from the docs):
```
from statsd import StatsClient
statsd = StatsClient()
statsd.incr('MyApplication')
```

I would argue that being able to increase/decrease by using `+=` and `-=` is more obvious but you have the choice available. If you wish, `counter.increment()` is also available.

In the end it mostly comes down to preference :)
If you are using Django you can also take a look at another project of mine which uses this one internally: https://github.com/WoLpH/django-statsd

Also, under OS X, semaphore handles (as returned by `sem_open()`) are file descriptors, so in the case where `stdin` has been closed, 0 can be a valid return from `sem_open()`. My code assumes that if the pointer to the semaphore is 0 (NULL), it's invalid, but that's not the case.

AFAICT, OS X is alone in using file descriptors for semaphore handles. OS X's closest living cousin (FreeBSD) doesn't exhibit this behavior, and neither does Linux. Their return from `sem_open()` is an ordinary pointer to a chunk of memory.

You can do this in XlsxWriter the same way that you would would do it in Excel: [Technique 1: Use a picture in a header to mimic a watermark](https://support.office.com/en-in/article/Mimic-a-watermark-in-Excel-5881607e-d76a-43a0-9ff5-6d63653f12bf).

```python
import xlsxwriter
```

Thanks for the detailed report.

The reason that the row is hidden is that the program is setting the row height to 0. Here:

```python
    worksheet.set_row(1, 0, myformat)
```
If you want to set the format but not set the row height then you should use `None` for the height:

```python
    worksheet.set_row(1, None, myformat)
```

Yes, though the ``before_content`` and ``after_content`` methods are part of the domain API so I don't think we can change the signature. However, we can probably assign it temporarily as a member of the directive.

> Is it possible to make ``cpp:struct``? I reached out to the sphinx dev list for counsel on how to implement it, but currently it is not possible to document C++ structs.

Sure, I'll add it soon. Classes and structs are the same so the difference will probably only be which class key is displayed in front of the declaration, and which is shown in the index.

the main problem is not that the entire XML dom gets loaded at once. that is fine and efficient for access to it and even large projects seldom have more than 100 MB of plain XML (that is smaller in RAM when parsed). The main problem is that `minidom` is used for parsing, which is cluttered with memory leaks and blows it up more then ten-fold in RAM.

I suspect the root cause is that you show the icon before [run](https://github.com/moses-palmer/pystray/blob/master/lib/pystray/_base.py#L160) has been called. There are no tests for this.

Managing the icon requires that the icon main loop is running. `run` provides the `setup` parameter, which allows you to pass a callback to be executed once the icon is ready; the is a suitable location to show the icon.

_pystray 0.12_ added a default implementation for `setup` that simply showed the icon.

Hi guys, thanks for your great work looking into this. `tqdm` should not fail when it fails to detect your system, it should fallback to a sensible default. I'll add a patch.

Secondly, are you sure it is unsafe for `__del__()` to call object methods? It seems unlikely that python would have the option of allowing a use-defined `__del__()` function which cannot safely access member objects for fear of them already being deleted.

As a slight tweak to what @jrollins says above, I'd also thought of being allowed to specify `-` instead of the `authorized_keys` file so e.g. you could pipe it to some file (and this would also play more-nicely with shell-script type things). Hi Alex, great find!

It looks like our [implementation](https://github.com/corydolphin/flask-cors/blob/master/flask_cors/__init__.py#L403) currently does not read and compare Access-Control-Request-Headers, but simply returns the list of allowed headers without performing comparison. This means we are slightly more permissive in the preflight phase than the spec dictates.

Practically, this means that it is possible for a client to submit a preflight request, asking to send a larger set of non-simple request headers than will actually be allowed during the request phase. 


To conform to the spec, it is relatively simple:
```
Let header field-names be the values as result of parsing the Access-Control-Request-Headers headers.

If method is not a case-sensitive match for any of the values in list of methods do not set any additional headers and terminate this set of steps.

Always matching is acceptable since the list of methods can be unbounded. //What we do today

If any of the header field-names is not a ASCII case-insensitive match for any of the values in list of headers do not set any additional headers and terminate this set of steps.
```

Did this cause an issue for you? Would love to hear more!

Cory

Hi @dkl3,

You'll want to use the checksum option. When the checksum option is turned on, an md5 checksum is generated of your local file and nothing is uploaded if a file with the same checksum already exists in the given item. Do note that this will not work if the item has pending tasks. This behavior is to avoid race conditions (i.e. if a file with a matching checksum existed in the item, but there was a task queued to delete that file, etc.).

In the CLI, this would like: 

```
$ ia upload <identifier> <file> --checksum
```

And, in Python:

```
>>> from internetarchive import upload
>>> r = upload('<identifier>', '<file>', checksum=True)
```

Another option is to use the delete option. When this option is turned on, your local files are deleted after they have been successful uploaded to archive.org. Of course, this only an option if you are in a position where deleting your local copies is an option. Anyways, using delete would look like:

```
$ ia upload <identifier> <file> --delete
```

And, in Python:

```
>>> from internetarchive import upload
>>> r = upload('<identifier>', '<file>', delete=True)
```

If you're running into a lot of 503 errors, you might find the retries parameter helpful (requests are retried for the specified amount of times when 5xx errors are encountered):

```
$ ia upload <identifier> <file> --retries 10
```

And, in Python:

```
>>> from internetarchive import upload
>>> r = upload('<identifier>', '<file>', retries=10)
```

Please email me at jake at archive.org if you have any more support questions, happy to help! : )

``moto`` doesn't really seem to buy me a whole lot. It only has partial support for many of the APIs, and I still need to manually define response data. Given that it's rather opaque and would add another dependency, I'm not going to pursue it.

``placebo`` on the other hand might be useful. It hooks into the events generated by a boto3 session, and can either record the responses to API calls or play them back. It *does* handle exceptions (non-200 responses) correctly. However, I see two main issues with it:

1. It records responses by service and API method, but serially (ordered by integers) beyond that. So playback of responses is order-dependent within each API method of each service. This means that any tests using it are order-dependent.
2. It doesn't record the arguments/parameters for the call, so it's a bit less useful as a troubleshooting tool.

So, formerly, calling render_component would do the following:
- send a request to the build server so that it can build a UMD module which exports the component, blocking until it sent back a signal
- serialise the props to json
- send the json and a path to the built file to a render server, blocking until it returns markup
- the serialised data and markup are bound to an object and returned from the function so that you can interact with it

When interacting with the object, the typical workflow would be:
- when injecting the markup into a template, it would wrap it in a `<span>` element containing a unique id attribute
- the `mount_js` method would:
  - inject a `<script>` element pointing to the built file - which exposes the component under a unique global variable
  - inject the serialized props
  - inject a little mount script which grabs the component and the props, finds the `<span>` element containing the unique id, and then mounts the component with the props into the `<span>` - in effect replacing the pre-rendered markup with markup controlled by React

I've omitted the details about webpack and build server, as it's long and messy. It was the most complicated and limiting part of the old codebase.

@Alireza2n thanks for your interest in this project.
First I'd like to point out that this library is a generic date library and is not tied to a specific framework, so decisions regarding how the library evolves should be agnostic to such contexts.

@slashmili I also find the described concern understandable. `date` object's configuration is based on the locale (either set by current process, or system default), but there maybe contexts where the caller needs to configure the locale directly without affecting the environment.

However IMO current implementation, while trying to solve the said problem, is fragile. Passing `locale` to `strftime` is changing the state of the object, which is prone to errors. This maybe described as an example of violation of [CQS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Command%E2%80%93query_separation).

```python
date = jdatetime.date(1394, 12, 4)
# date is using default locale
print date.strftime("%a, %d %b %Y", locale='fa_IR')
# from now on date is using fa_IR locale which is not a welcome side effect
```

I think a safer approach here is to configure the instance's `locale` on `__init__` (where we're currently doing so by calling `_is_fa_locale()` and validate the argument (at least the format, more on this later). Then providing a [property](https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#property) is more favorable than having a public attribute.
I understand there might be a concern with this approach, which is when the application did not initialize the `date` instance, say another library creates `date` objects (for example a Django wrapper, etc.) yet application still needs to format the `date` in a different locale. Although passing the `locale` to `strtime` may seem to solve the issue, for such cases I suggest the caller would clone the `date` object, and change the locale of the new instance and then call `strftime`, so still we won't need to pass the `locale` to `strftime`. 

I'd also suggest this `locale` parameter to follow Python standard library [locale format](https://docs.python.org/2/library/locale.html#locale.getlocale), which is a tuple of `(language code, encoding)` to provide an API more compatible with stdlib.

On a side note, having a mutable `locale` attribute on the `date` object, requires `strftime` to check for `locale` on each call, which means implementation of `_is_fa_locale()` would better avoid unnecessary operations, to keep this performance penalty minimum. So I'd defer getting process/system locales to only when they are actually required, wheres current implementation *always* calls both `_locale.getlocale()` and `_locale.getdefaultlocale()`, even when the object has an explicit `locale` set.

If you're having a case where the suggested approach won't be sufficient, could you please share the context?

The django migrations framework is a big, complicated piece of code.

To mirror it's functionality for serialized JSON representations of models
would be a equally hard task. Also, the performance would be terrible,
since each version to be migrated would need to be streamed from the
database to python, decoded from JSON, altered, re-encoded, then streamed
back into the database.

It would be a wonderful piece of functionality. It would also have
appalling performance and multiple the complexity of django-reversion a
hundredfold! :O

I figure that with the `previousData` attribute, users should be able to figure out how to create their own iterators. For example, here's a generator I made (from [this blog post](http://aguo.us/writings/spotify-billboard.html)) that returns the past 100 top singles:

```python
def unique_top_tracks_generator():
    seen_tracks = set()
    chart = billboard.ChartData('hot-100')  # Start at most recent

    while len(seen_tracks) < 100:
        top_track = chart[0]
        duplicate = top_track.spotifyLink in seen_tracks

        if not duplicate:
            seen_tracks.add(top_track.spotifyLink)
            yield top_track

        chart = billboard.ChartData('hot-100', date=chart.previousDate)

    raise StopIteration
```

Re: `RuntimeError('working outside of request context')`

http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.10/api/#flask.copy_current_request_context

> flask.copy_current_request_context(f)
> A helper function that decorates a function to retain the current request context. This is useful when working with greenlets. The moment the function is decorated a copy of the request context is created and then pushed when the function is called.

> Example:

```python
import gevent
from flask import copy_current_request_context

@app.route('/')
def index():
    @copy_current_request_context
    def do_some_work():
        # do some work here, it can access flask.request like you
        # would otherwise in the view function.
        ...
    gevent.spawn(do_some_work)
    return 'Regular response'

```

@jimni, I traced down the difference between S2L and ES2L. ES2L is using semi blocking page-ready check on keywords that internally called ``Wait Until Angular Ready`` keyword. There are two options to skip this behavior. The first can be done globally during library import by specifying `block_until_page_ready=${false}`. The second approach is using the new `skip_ready` argument that I just added in release 0.6.2 . I modified your test-suite example as below. This should give you exactly S2L behavior.

```
    Open Browser  http://nikitin.su/s2l_test.html  ff

    Choose Ok On Next Confirmation
    Click Link  Delete  skip_ready=${true}

    Open Browser  http://nikitin.su/s2l_test.html  ff
```

```
diff --git a/hgwatchman/__init__.py b/hgwatchman/__init__.py
--- a/hgwatchman/__init__.py
+++ b/hgwatchman/__init__.py
@@ -170,6 +170,8 @@
     if normalize:
         foldmap = dict((normcase(k), k) for k in results)
```

Here is some proof. ``adopt_user`` works nicely but ``adopt_role`` does not. Can someone shed light on what happens? I consider that a bug.

```python
def test_adopt_roles_is_superweird(self):
    from AccessControl import Unauthorized
    from plone.app.testing import logout
    api.content.create(
        type='Document',
        container=self.portal,
        title='private Doc',
    )
    api.user.create(
        username='superman',
        email='superman@krypton.gov',
        password='secret',
        roles=('Member', 'Manager'),
    )
    api.user.create(
        username='punyman',
        email='punyman@earth.gov',
        password='secret',
        roles=('Member'),
    )
    catalog = api.portal.get_tool('portal_catalog')

    # The new object can be found
    brains = catalog(review_state='private')
    self.assertEqual(len(brains), 1)

    logout()

    # As anonymous there are no results
    brains = catalog(review_state='private')
    self.assertEqual(len(brains), 0)

    # Anonymous canot access the object
    brains = catalog.unrestrictedSearchResults(review_state='private')
    self.assertEqual(len(brains), 1)
    self.assertRaises(Unauthorized, brains[0].getObject)

    with api.env.adopt_roles(roles=['Manager']):

        # This should return the the brains but does not. Why?
        brains = catalog(review_state='private')
        self.assertEqual(len(brains), 0)

        # But accessing the object from the brain now works!
        brains = catalog.unrestrictedSearchResults(review_state='private')
        self.assertEqual(len(brains), 1)
        obj = brains[0].getObject()
        self.assertEqual(api.content.get_state(obj), 'private')

    # adopt_user works nicely :-) 
    with api.env.adopt_user(username='superman'):
        brains = catalog(review_state='private')
        self.assertEqual(len(brains), 1)

    with api.env.adopt_user(username='punyman'):
        brains = catalog(review_state='private')
        self.assertEqual(len(brains), 0)

    # When granting manager-role to punyman the result is still empty :-(
    # In the next request the brain would be found. Why?
    api.user.grant_roles(username='punyman', roles=['Manager'])
    brains = catalog(review_state='private')
    self.assertEqual(len(brains), 0)
```

Revisiting this again... **What is/are the role/responsibilities of the maintainers?**

### Current Technical State

I've never maintained a project, and quite honestly Luigi is the first major project to which I've contributed. I feel that I know how to use it for my cases (although it may not be the intended Luigi pattern), which means I have pretty good knowledge of general execution (in terms of writing jobs) and modules like S3, Redshift, FTP, etc. (which are largely within contrib). The areas of Luigi I feel weak are more around the core functionality (details of the process of execution, including the central scheduler), the visualizer (and javascript in general), and testing (not necessarily Luigi-specific, just inexperienced at writing tests). Also, the relationship between Luigi, travis-ci, tox, readthedocs, and pypi are understood on a macro level (i.e. i understand the role each piece plays), but are unfamiliar to me on terms of implementation.

I have enjoyed contributing and helping others in the community. So long as I'm in my current position (which is the for the foreseeable future), I'll be working with Luigi on a more-or-less daily basis.

### Goal

While I'm here, I'd like to learn as much as I can, while also refining and expanding this project.

@jcampbell , my concern is:

`Marshmallow` Nested Schemas are not "related data", just nested data 

Example:
```python
class Address(Schema):
    zip_code = String(required=True)
    country = String(missing='Brazil')
    state = String(required=True)
    city = String(required=True)
    district = String(required=True)
    street = String(required=True)
    number = String(required=True)
    complement = String(missing='')

class Person(Schema):
    address_list = Nested(Address, many=True)

class PersonModel(ModelBase):
    address_list = Column(JSON(), default='[]', nullable=False, server_default='[]')
```
in this example, there is no need "address_list" to be a second table, its a nested data in the PersonModel table

my concern is that your patch could interpret the input of this schema as sqlalchemy relationship.

I do agreed that be able to create nested relationships is nice ( I even have a perfect case for this )
but this new feature must be iron solid, to avoid undesired behaviours, should only affect relationships and I think it would be nice if I could optout this featured on some `Resources` ( maybe as arg for the data_layer )

By default, botocore does not use signature version 4 for S3 unless the region *only* supports signature version 4 (such as eu-central-1).  This check is based on the metadata for a region (for example: https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/data/endpoints.json#L685-L690).  If your using a region that's not specified in that file you'll either need to explicitly set the signature version as you're doing or override the `endpoints.json` file.

No, two nets, a direct and an isolated network can't have the same name in a given ovdc. But if we just use the records to figure out the href, we run the risk of blurring the boundaries between isolated and direct networks. Let me give an example, If we use records to figure out the href, then we are not filtering on the type of network, since record doesn't have that info. Thanks for the bug report. Support for decimal types was added in 1.0.0 and this required a change in the `VARIANT` structure. I have a feeling this is what is causing the problem.

Prior to the change, `VARIANT` was a structure containing a union. Now it is a structure containing a union, which itself contains a structure with a union. This change brings the comtypes `VARIANT` definition closer to the [C++ definition](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms221627%28v=vs.85%29.aspx).

I suspect this is probably an issue with pypy's ctypes implementation. A quick google search indicates that the pypy implementation initializes fields differently than the CPython one (see [here](https://gist.github.com/marianoguerra/3281751)).

It's worth raising a bug report on pypy about this. I'll see if I can do something on this end, but any help you or the community can give with this would help speed this along.

> I think you misunderstand the global registry issue.

This is possible.

> Include library A which registers a global default for `SomeType` (and likely does not expose whatever code it has to register it to a different registry)
> Include library B which registers a global default for `SomeType` (and likely does not expose whatever code it has to register it to a different registry)

At least for this PR, this code does not work that way. Here, library B would not register _an additional_ hook, it would overwrite the hook that was registered by library A. There's only ever one dispatch registered for `SomeType` objects - subsequent registrations overwrite previous registrations.

> Your application needs to pick between library A and library B's custom serialization for SomeType depending on which third party service you're talking to. You're stuck.

Nope, you aren't stuck. This would only be an issue if you cannot choose the order in which libraries are imported. For this PR, whichever library was loaded last would overwrite the previous registrations for the same types.

Also, this is easily fixed - just register your own function after library A and library B are both imported. You can make this guaranteed by:

```python
import libraryA  # Ensure libraryA is imported (subsequent imports are cached in CPython)
import libraryB  # Ensure libraryB is imported
import json

chosen_library = libraryA  # or libraryB

@json.JSONEncoder.default.register(SomeType)
def _(encoder, sometype_obj):
    return chosen_library.encode_json(sometype_obj)
```

Furthermore, I think this is largely a red herring. This feature is most useful for frameworks, and it's very unlikely users will be using more than one framework at a time.

But, for the small amount of users who may be using both (maybe they're transitioning a Django project to a Flask or Falcon project and for some reason need to encode things differently), they can still force the framework to use a specific encoder by either passing in their own `default` argument or by subclassing `simplejson.JSONEncoder` and using that.

> This style of code is what's typically referred to as monkeypatching and is not a very common Python idiom, since it's not composable.

That's true, and that's a valid criticism, but all I was illustrating is that users can workaround the fact that both `json` and `simplejson` need a common registry when they are both used in a project. I'm not sure how common that is. You would have a better idea of how common that is.

[np](https://github.com/sindresorhus/np) uses `--no-package-lock` (and the Yarn equivalent). The idea there is to run your tests with all your npm dependencies unlocked to the latest compatible ranges, explicitly ignoring the lockfile.

That specific use case is possibly less relevant given the existence of tox (and the difficulty of building anything like np for Python), even so for something like a library, it sort of makes sense to have both a locked-down set of dependencies so that people jumping in don't have to deal with a broken build environment, and to allow people to test against latest compatible dependencies before e.g. a release.
The last one you’ll need to submit to pip (notpip is a patched version of it), otherwise I have no objections.

Well, the harm is this - if it's an implementation detail, something Setuptools does not wish to guarantee, then adding a test for it is confusing. If the behavior is meant to be guaranteed, then it should have a test, but if it's a detail that's subject to change, it probably shouldn't have a test.

In fact, if Setuptools' declared behavior is that the order of the details is not guaranteed, then the tests are in fact wrong and should instead assert that the values are present in any order (and not in a specific order).

Most importantly, this decision shouldn't be made lightly, locking in an implementation detail, simply because that's the current expectation.

It's not even clear to me that pbr should have had any expectation that there was deterministic ordering of these values. Just because their tests relied on it doesn't mean that it was a regression in Setuptools or that any package was right to rely on that behavior.

And since the guaranteed interface of Setuptools isn't rigorously defined, there may be no good answer to that matter, so it makes sense herein to rigorously define it one way or another. In the past, I would have leaned toward not providing deterministically-ordered output, but several tickets in the project since have specifically been working toward generating deterministic builds, suggesting this PR does make sense.

3DES uses an "encrypt decrypt encrypt" model (EDE) so you can do single DES encryption and decryption with pyca/cryptography by doing the following:

```python
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.ciphers import Cipher, algorithms, modes
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
3des_key = des_key * 3 # just concat the key 3x
cipher = Cipher(algorithms.3DES(3des_key), modes.ECB(), backend=default_backend())
encryptor = cipher.encryptor()
# not using padding here so you'll need to make sure your input is a multiple of block size or else add padding
ct = encryptor.update(b"encrypt!") + encryptor.finalize()
```

This will work (albeit 3x as slow as single DES).

Ideally you'd just drop LM support though as it's beyond ancient.

I think the fundamental thing you're getting at here, which is quite useful, is that `SimpleConsumer.consume()` needs some knowledge of whether a rebuild has been triggered between when it starts executing and when it finishes. The use of a lock for this, however, doesn't make a lot of sense to me. Since there's only one place in this code where you're acquiring your new lock, you're effectively using it as a boolean flag instead of a lock. For this case, I think a `self._cluster.handler.Event` is more appropriate. Instead of acquiring the lock, you can `set()` the event. `consume()` can check `my_event.is_set()` and return early if `True`.

The definition of "fully supported" isn't locked down, which is probably the main issue.

I'd say we fully support boolean images as Python objects. However, because the common image formats don't support this dtype, we're left with compromises any time they have to touch the disk.

Automatically bouncing such images to full-range [0, 255] on save makes me uncomfortable. We'd be changing users' data. People working with those may well have considered them like labeled images, since `img.astype(bool) == 1` resolves, and there could be (a lot of) code out there which assumes `img = io.imread('./path/to/img.png') == 1` is going to preserve their data "values" like it always has. We're used to playing fast and loose with types in Python for better or worse and this workflow, well, works.

I noticed this a long time ago, back in around version 0.9, but thought it was working as intended. My assumption was that this was a deliberate design decision along the lines of what Cython does with `bint` - use the next largest supported dtype where necessary. With any viewer supporting an automatic window, the saved images appear just fine. Is it really a bug?

as so many issues in RDFLib this is historically grown... we still serialize with the old `7bit` ascii ntriples 1.0 format by default. ntriples 1.1 still allows this: https://www.w3.org/TR/n-triples/#grammar-production-IRIREF (so it's not a bug, but more compatible).

There's also already a [NT1.1Serializer](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/plugins/serializers/nt.py#L36). Still this issue shows, that as mentioned in #400 and #450 we certainly should go forward, make NT1.1 the default and write utf-8 encoded ntriples. It should still however be possible to generate NT1.0 for older systems...

OK, if we use ```max_flux``` values of (34.70, 32.80, 32.55, 33.67) then we lose 5% of the galaxies that would pass the S/N cuts, and eliminate ~44% of the ones that fail the S/N cuts (or ~90% of the ones that fail the S/N<100 cut).  Those aren't linear in PSF FWHM but I think that's because of some kind of funny trade-off that comes from the noise variance depending on FWHM.  And btw these are the values of ```gal_flux``` using sersicfit, even for those galaxies for which the object is actually rendered using bulgefit.

There's not really a good ```min_flux``` cut to get rid of the ones that fail the minimum S/N cut; at low flux, we'd need a complicated flux vs. radius cut (since it's sort of the effective average surface brightness that determines the S/N) and we don't lose so many things at that end, anyway, so I don't think it matters much.  Plus those aren't as expensive to render as the high-SNR ones.  

And I don't think we need a ```max_hlr``` cut once we have the ```max_flux``` cut.

Do you want to try this and confirm that this seems to help without losing too many objects?

diam_sq_ratio = (8.4**2) / (2.4**2)
# exposure time of LSST in secs compared to 1 sec for HST
exp_time = 16
gal_lsst = gal_hst.withScaledFlux(flux_ratio=diam_sq_ratio * exp_time)

# LSST PSF (Parameters based on the lsst.yaml)

fwhm = 0.67 # Value depends on wavelength of monochromatic PSF
# Constructing atmospheric PSF
base_atmos = galsim.Kolmogorov(fwhm=fwhm) # 500 nm
lsst_atmos = galsim.ChromaticAtmosphere(base_atmos, 500, zenith_angle=0 * galsim.degrees)
# Optical Component
lam = 700 # nanometers
diam = 8.4 # meters
lam_over_diam = 0.017 # (700 nm / 8.4 m) * 206265 = 0.017 arcsec

First, let's clear the air. None of these issues are intentional misrepresentations. The world changed since that statement was originally made. And we're fixing those problems right now.

Stepping back from this particular issue, I don't believe we've been given the benefit of the doubt here. Some particular folks appear to belive this was malicious on our part. It was not. Just like you would have for your own self, I'll challenge you to assume good motivations on the part of others.

And know that your reputation follows you online. Especially here on GitHub. Folks in the community learn who the bad actors are and don't like dealing with them, no matter how smart they are and what they know. Seriously, think about it. If you think this might be written to you, it probably is. Your involvement is honestly appreciated. But please be polite, respectful, and assume good intentions of others.

For context, when Font Awesome came out 4 years ago, making icons as fonts work with screen readers was a major priority. And it worked well. Since then, the world changed. Screen readers changed, but Font Awesome didn't keep up.

Going forward, vetted accessibility issues will be given priority for any subsequent release. It's not been enough of a priority lately, and we're changing that. 

We really do appreciate you experience, knowledge, and expertise. Thank you for helping out the project, and for helping the literally millions of folks that use this.

After some juggling, the `__slots__` size is reported along with other `__class__`-level attributes.  Note, (a) that is the size of the `__slots__` _definition_ in the `__class__` and (b)  `__class__` attributes are considered _code_ in `asizeof`, meaning keyword argument `code=True` is required to get those sizes.  Example:

```
import sys
print(sys.version)
from pympler import asizeof

FOOS = ("foo",)

class Foo(object):
  __slots__ = FOOS

  def __init__(self):
    self.foo = FOOS

print('Foo: %s' % sys.getsizeof(Foo()))
print(asizeof.asized(Foo, code=True, detail=1).format())
print(asizeof.asized(Foo(), detail=1).format())

BARS = ("foo", "bar")

class Bar(object):
  __slots__ = BARS

  def __init__(self):
    self.foo = BARS
    self.bar = BARS

print('Bar: %s' % sys.getsizeof(Bar()))
print(asizeof.asized(Bar, code=True, detail=1).format())
print(asizeof.asized(Bar(), detail=1).format())
```

I'd be concerned with disabling that specific test because it's a security feature.

In that test, mutual authentication is required, an http error occurs, and mutual authentication is made to fail. 

When mutual authentication fails (when required) on otherwise successful responses, we raise an exception. When mutual authentication fails (when required) on an otherwise failed response, we return the response, but sanitize it to protect the user -- they wanted mutual authentication, it couldn't be provided, so they shouldn't get the response content.

@dhermes Our goals for testing snippets inline:

- Snippets have no syntax errors.  We could mock *everything* to satisfy this case.
- Snippets calls to each API match their signatures.  Here, we would have to mock things at the connection / stub / helper level.  We would still not catch stuff where the values passed in matched the names of expected arguments, but failed to marshal properly, or match what the back-end mandates in some other way.
- Snippets show actually correct processing of results as they would be returned by the API.  Here we would be required to mock *everything* about the back-end for each API.

I don't believe we can reasonably expect to do the second or third options inside the test setup / teardown facilities provided by Sphinx.  Exercising the snippets in a `system-test`-like environment, against the real APIs, seems like the only viable choice.

Yeah, it looks like all of the lint checks run even if you use the `lint.checkstyle` goal. Oh well, I like the more explicit goal for the tests anyway.

> On the command line a trailing or bare `:` means glob all targets here. With this change it would mean something different inside BUILD files; ie: address syntax becomes context-dependent.

I just added a bit of clarification, but the only additional target spec this pr allows is `':'` -- `a/b:` for example is still an error. I actually don't know if I see the context-dependence for `':'` or `:other_target_name` specs -- on the command line that would be interpreted as relative to the build root, but in a BUILD file refers to the target in the same BUILD file, which seems pretty consistent. 

> One of the main points of adding support for eliding the name of the default module was less boiler-plate for the common case of 1-1-1 BUILD files. In the case where there is more than one target in the BUILD file and targets within depend on the default target it seems like a minimal burden to just explicitly assign the default target its name explicity with a `name='<parent dir name>'`.

I think I definitely agree with this and the idea of discouraging multiple targets in BUILD files in general. Thinking it over again, I think the error message when a nonexistent target name is given for some directory is very useful, so I'm less worried about the difficulty of reconnecting the references to the default target if a directory is renamed (especially since all references to targets in that directory elsewhere would have to be changed).

I think right now I'm with you because it's good to require explicit target naming if you take the step of defining multiple targets in the same BUILD file. I will leave this open for a while and then close it.

When you call `keras.utils.multi_gpu_model(model, gpus=gpus)`, the `model` refer to as `keras.models.Sequential object` and it sets the appropriate `context`. Here in this case it's `[gpu(0), gpu(1), gpu(2), gpu(3)]` if the number of GPUs is 4.

During compile phase, Sequential build() method(`def build() in models.py`) creates a MXNet model object(`keras.backend.mxnet_backend.get_model`) and this MXNet model set context as default context(`[gpu(0)]`) and calls `self.model.compile()`.

So, there is a separate context for each model heirarchy and both are not in sync. First context is set during `multi_gpu_model` for `keras.models.Sequential object` (Which set correct context) and second context is set during `build()` for  `keras.backend.mxnet_backend.get_model`(which set incorrect context).



Note:

```python
self: <keras.models.Sequential object at 0x7f3002ad9630>

self.model: <keras.backend.mxnet_backend.get_model.<locals>.Model object at 0x7f2ffb4ec9b0> 
```
Any work around for now?

I'm trying to write to storage where the postgresql user does not have ownership permissions. The attempts to make files and directories owner-accessible only rather than owner and group thus doesn't work for me -- the ownership is actually coerced away from the user who writes the files, meaning that content created 0700 in the filesystem in question isn't readable by the user who created it (directories can't be cd'd into by the user who created them, etc).

So, I'm pondering an adequate workaround for that -- but since it's a bizarre corner case unlikely to impact the vast majority of users, folding any such workaround into this patch strikes me as an unreasonable expansion of scope.

Confirm very slow work on large or complicated (lots of rowspans/colspans) tables. I was using [this](https://github.com/m9psy/html_table_to_excel/blob/master/main.py#L60-L97) code to generate table from html code. Table size is ~ 150*8 and it taken over the 20 seconds to complete.
The add_row method only add rows at the end of table, but I need insert some rows into the middle of table , so help!

In looking at this a bit more, all of the time is taken up by the table._cells call which happens every time I fetch a row.cells.  To retrieve a row, _cells has to iterate through every cell in the entire table to deal with merged cells, and doesn't have a mechanism to regenerate only on change.

As a work-around, since I just need a simple table I'm fetching all the cells once and indexing the rows:
```python
COLUMNS = 7
table = document.add_table(rows=1000, columns=COLUMNS)
table_cells = table._cells
for i in range(ROWS):
    row_cells = table_cells[i*COLUMNS:(i+1)*COLUMNS]
    #Add text to row_cells
```
This takes around 4 seconds to populate 1000 rows.

Thanks for the request, here are my answers:

1. Times-stamping reports is definitely doable; I'll most likely add a --timestamp parameter that defaults to the current time in UTC and can be overwritten with whatever users choose. Reports and config files would have the timestamp as a suffix too (e.g. report-<profile_name>-timestamp.html). Does this sound ok?

2. Loading previous reports in a single HTML report would be doable but I'd rather not implement this feature. Once reports are time-stamped, opening two (or more) reports in separate tabs will be comparable and it will help avoiding crazy-sized DOMs that may become irresponsive when multiple runs are in memory for the same DOM.

3. I will not add support for trend because I want to keep Scout2 as a security auditing tool and adding trends/charts does not add value from an auditor's point of view.

Note to self, as after investigation it may take me some time to implement the solution:

On peer update, we have two lists of routes to compare.

Checking for an element in a list is O(n) so with large configuration this part of the code could take ages. As it is not async, it could cause BGP sessions to drop. The solution to prevent this was to mark all current routes as withdrawn, and then re-announce all the routes. Hammer - but works.

On 3.4, the announce after the withdraw would cause the withdraw to be removed and only the announce would be sent. We found out that this optimisation could cause issue, so on master, all the route will be explicitly withdrawn and then re-announced. 

This optimisation could be re-considered for this case - re-adding the code but only enabling it when we are doing this double loop work.

One solution would be to have the routes in the peer as dict and not list. Then take all the new routes and remove them from the old dict(). What would be left would be the routes to remove.
If a route was removed then the route does not need to be announced. The route not removed then would make our announcement.

That solution should be fast enough ..

I think the billing issue I said could theoretically exist in the PR thread does already exist.

Imagine two instances fire up due to a scaling event and both have a cron to check they have an Elastic IP every minute. Once booted they will both race for the same Elastic IP as shown in @brodygov's log.

With an EC2-Classic account (pre-2014) only one instance will manage to associate and the other will error. This will incur a $0.10 charge. In the second minute the second instance will associate and incur another $0.10 charge. Total charges are $0.20.

With an EC2-VPC-only account (2014+) both instances could successfully associate in the first minute and incur $0.20 of charges. In the second minute the instance that was stolen from will associate again and incur another $0.10 charge. Total charges are $0.30.

Not a big difference but it escalates quickly. If the bad luck was repeated with 10 instances it would take 10 minutes to properly associate every instance and the difference by then is $1.00 vs $5.50.

I would definitely upgrade to boto 3 and explicitly set AllowReassociation to False for everyone.

If requested features that would require reassociation (e.g. #13 or #19) are ever implemented then they should be done so carefully.

Please keep in mind that Python 2.6 is still alive and will remain so until the year 2020 due to [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6](https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/errata/#Life_Cycle_Dates). So you may not want to drop support for that.

There's already a pretty nice [pull request](https://github.com/python-diamond/Diamond/pull/259) that only drops support for 2.5 and still gets py3k working (almost). I really like this approach because it provides a big advantage (=py3k) with hardly any disadvantage (=drop only 2.5). In my experience this works out pretty well, even though you miss out on some of the cool features of 2.7.

And while some external components may not be compatible with py3k, officially dropping support for Python 2.5 would be the first step towards that goal. Because trying to get code to run under both 2.5 and py3k is pretty futile.

@aj-may I ran into a couple issues when trying to solve this with a single unit, neither of which seemed solvable.

One is that the shutdown unit will still need to have the store components listed in the After, and since they wont exist in a stateless platform or dataplane node, the unit will not execute the ExecStop. This is the behavior I observed, I never found a way to avoid that.

Another issue with a single unit is that once the graceful stop unit is enabled and started, Wants will attempt to start the services listed there, which would not be desirable in a stateless platform.

With two units, the main etcd and store units could Want their cleanup units and start them when they start. That was intended to be part 2 of this, once its been vetted.

It would be great to get your feedback on this approach, thanks much!
