The way I have reworked the `activate`/`deactivate` logic means a few things:

1. The `deactivate` script only deactivates an environment if it finds that the environment variable CONDA_PREFIX exists (this is set in `activate` and likewise unset in `deactivate`). In other words, the root environment cannot be removed from the user's PATH unless they are explicitly doing something weird like setting CONDA_PREFIX (in which case I would think it's the user's fault for getting weird results).
2. The `activate` script always prepends the activating environment to the PATH. So in the case of `. activate root` a normal user will expect to see the root path(s) listed twice.
2. The `deactivate` script only removes the first occurrence of the environment's paths to deactivate. So again if the user activated root only the first occurrence of the root path will be removed.

So in conclusion, yes, if a user follows the recommended installation of Conda and appends the root environment to PATH they will be stacking the activating environment on top of the root environment. If they don't append the Conda root to their PATH then activating an environment will not stack it with the root. BUT consequently that also means that they cannot easily call the `activate`/`deactivate` scripts especially since these scripts (and their helper scripts) are currently designed to be available on the user's PATH.

> Oh.. sorry for not checking. Shouldn't conda-env also get updated when I do a conda update conda though..?

Yes and no. :-) Previous versions of `conda` had a strategy of performing the minimal set of updates to bring an environment up to date. So if upgrading only to conda 2.5.0 was sufficient, that's what it would do. 

We've now adjusted the algorithm so that, instead of minimizing the *total version bumps*, it just minimizes the *number of packages changed*---but updates those packages to the maximum. So *that* would indeed pick up the latest conda-env.

tl;dr The conda provided compilers required a change to Python which must be present in the base environment.  Periodically updating Python in the base environment is a good idea.

A bit of background: Starting with the release of Anaconda 5.0, conda packages from Anaconda, Inc are compiled using up-to-date compilers for better performance and security.  The compilers themselves  are provided in conda packages.  The theano package requires these compilers as the library requires working compilers at run-time.

The messages are coming from the package which activates the compilers.  Setting **CC**, **CFLAGS**, etc are needed to use these compilers but can be a bit of a surprise. We added the messages to provide a heads up on then modification to the environmental variables.

Python stores the configuration of the build environment (compiler paths, CFLAGS, etc) when it is compiled.  These configuration are then reused when Python builds a C extension.  This creates a bit of a problem, Python is built with these new compilers, but they may not be available.  In such cases  building C extensions would fail.  

To accommodate this, the Python in Anaconda uses a generic set of build configuration by default which will work with most system provide compilers.  If the conda provided compilers are installed, which we do encourage if you are going to build C extensions, then the configuration for these compilers is used.  This switch if controlled by setting the **_PYTHON_SYSCONFIGDATA_NAME** environment variable.  

This behavior has worked well but there is one downside. Python's that were built before the switch to these new compilers do not include the logic required to make this switch and will error out if **_PYTHON_SYSCONFIG_DATA_NAME** is set.  If such a Python is installed in the base conda environment you get an error like you saw.  

The fix is relatively easy, update the Python to a version which knows about the new compiles.  This also gives you any bug fixes and improvement that have been made to Python.  Periodically updating the Python in the base environment is a great idea. That said, we prefer to not make these kind of breaking changes and try to minimize the instances where they are needed.  Updating Python in the base environment is recommended but we try very had to not make it a requirement.  In this case it was the best option.

if you use Anaconda, you can use  `conda install -c hesi_m tensorflow ` to install tensorflow 1.9.0 for CPU 

if you specifically use Keras also, you can use  `conda install -c hesi_m keras` which installs Keras 2.2.0 in accompany with everything including tensorflow 1.9.0

It is better to make a fresh anaconda environment for your experiments

I've written up some initial user stories for what we want out of "features 3.0"

---

As a conda user:

- I want conda to find the optimal binary packages for my cpu and gpu. 
- I want the ability to choose between gpu and cpu versions of a package.
- I want to pick a specific package from a variety of cpu-optimized packages, so that I can have explicit specificity of my environment. 
- I want to create a debug environment. (mutex on Windows but not on others)
- I want to choose the python interpreter from a variety of alternatives (e.g. cpython, pypy).
- I want user-friendly expressiveness.

  *     conda install python=2.7 py-interpreter=cpython
  *     conda install python=2.7 py-interpreter=pypy
  *     conda install numpy blas=openblas gpu=cuda8

- I want tools to be able to expand information contained in the hash of a build string, for example to see what cuda versions are available for a particular package.

As a package recipe author:

- I want a way to force exclusivity of packages other than package name, specifically for when I know there will be overlapping paths and functionality.
- I want to have a clear mental model of the difference between when my package needs to depend on another package and when my package needs to declare a "feature" requirement.

Running on Windows 8.1 32-bit, filesystem NTFS, on magnetic (i.e. non-SSD) hard drive.

# Analysis
I am coming to believe that the issue lies with the windows api when the processes' working directory is different from the directory of the file system operations in NTFS junction subdirectories:

Setup:
Disk 0: NTFS system partition on SSD mounted as `C:\`
Disk 1: NTFS data partition on magnetic HDD

Disk 1 is mounted as a NTFS junction point in `C:\Users\Me\Documents` and also separately as `Z:\`.

**Observing general Windows problems with cross-drive NTFS junctions:**
Bcolz rootdir: `C:\Users\Me\Documents\abc\def\mydata.bcolz`
1. Working directory: `C:\Users\Me\Documents\abc\def`: executing `rmdir /Q /S mydata.bcolz` is a IO bound operation
2. Working directory: `C:\Users\Me\Documents\abc\def\xyz`: executing `rmdir /Q /S ..\dailyPrices.bcolz` is a CPU bound operation

In the first case, the `cmd.exe` process hardly consumes any CPU time, while in the latter it consumes 100% of one core.

But note:
3. Working directory: `Z:\abc\def\xyz`: executing `rmdir /Q /S ..\dailyPrices.bcolz` is again an IO bound operation!

**The issue is further illustrated by profiler runs with different rootdir parameters:**
*Rootdir in working directory (in subdirectory of a NTFS junction):*
```

```

*Rootdir not in working directory but in subdirectory of a NTFS junction:*

*Rootdir not in working directory and NOT in subdirectory of a NTFS junction:*
```
In [40]: %prun db = bcolz.ctable.fromhdf5('mydata.h5', '/mydata/table', rootdir='..\\mydata.bcolz', cparams=bcolz.cparams(cname='zlib', clevel=9))

Hi @IamVincentLin, I've looked into your scenario.

You create a URL path map, with associated rules, but for this to appear in the portal, you must create an application gateway rule (a request routing rule) which actually wires up that path map to a listener. So you would have something like:
`az network application-gateway rule create -g {rg} --gateway-name {ag} -n {path-rule} --http-listener {listener} --url-path-map {path-map}`

Then it should appear (and more importantly, function!).  There are some service side quirks related to path maps and rules that the portal tries to streamline, whereas the CLI tends to expose the raw API. I'll split this into two separate issues (1 for improving this experience and 1 for fixing the name/ID bug).

If your path map rules don't appear after wiring them up with a request routing rule, please let me know.

Based on feedback in my Homebrew PR, the Homebrew install does not use a virtual environment.
https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/pull/17344#issuecomment-326828536

Instead, the PYTHONPATH is set to the correct site-packages directory manually (which is what the virtual env would have done).
```
$ cat $(which az)
#!/usr/bin/env bash
export PYTHONPATH="/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.17/libexec/lib/python3.6/site-packages"
python3.6 -m azure.cli "$@"
```

I took a quick look at how to do job timeouts. I didn't realize Python's `threading` module deliberately has no way to stop a thread. This [StackExchange answer](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/323972/is-there-any-way-to-kill-a-thread-in-python) has some hacky ways to do it; a call to `ctypes.pythonapi.PyThreadState_SetAsyncExc` or maybe `pthread_kill` to force the thread to exit. That's a little spooky but maybe OK for an emergency.

The other choice is to switch jobs to `multiprocessing`, processes have a `terminate()` method. I like this option better. I mentioned somewhere that multiprocessing interacts strangely with logging but it's [pretty benign](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html#logging), the docs say that the worst that happens is two messages hit a Handler at the same time and they get intermixed in a logfile. I think even that's not a problem given Mike's strategy for per-job logging. But I'm a little confused about the multiprocessing environment, will require careful testing.

I note that I've run 600+ sources twice now and only ever encountered 3 that hung forever. And those 3 are now fixed by the HTTP timeout I committed. So while being able to stop a runaway job is a good idea, it may not be *essential* right now in order to do useful work.

A good solution perhaps if the C code in your CPython project is absolutely needed and the range of user platforms is limited and well-known in advance.

Using C code, especially for LibYAML is a security issue over using pure Python since any and all C code is at particular risk of memory security issues. See e.g. [this paper](http://www.fort-knox.org/files/mallocjournal.pdf). In practice, this means that someone writing malformed YAML could use that to execute arbitrary code. This kind of vulnerability happens now, to the best, and to popular widely used libraries. Moreover, [the YAML spec](http://yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html) is notoriously complex. For something potentially operating at the center of software development in an organization (e.g., on a CI host), that's unattractive.

We need to know is the connection secured or not.

It's a general principle that user configuration shouldn't prevent your entire application from working at all. It doesn't 'break' the timing behaviour, it changes it. Undoubtedly if we change it back, another group of people will complain about things that break.

We have to load it at a different time to catch the exception, I believe, because of the way Javascript module loading works. We make one `require()` call which loads multiple modules. If any of them fail, the whole load fails. So in order to catch exceptions from custom.js and carry on with the rest of the code, we need to separate loading that file from loading the other code.

What is actually the problem here?

* "Too big ipynb files" or "too much in RAM" because every plot includes jquery/... again -> solved by properly labeling dependencies in the over-the-wire messages and deduplicating them in the frontend
* "too much send over the wire" -> not solved by deduplicating in the frontend and must get a solution in the kernel (or in a caching webserver if it acts as a proxy between kernel and frontend)

If the latter is a problem (and one assumes that the kernelserver/webserver and the kernel are on the same host), then the above (labeled dependencies in the message) plus a proxy which does caching and replaces dependencies with hashes which are then loaded from the webserver could work:

-> kernel sends message with css/html labeled as dependency
-> proxy/"webserver" replaces dependency with hashes
-> frontend finds hashes -> requests content from webserver
-> webserver sends content for hash or error message
-> frontend includes hash and dependency in json and sends the complete stuff to be saved (or the frontend sends only hashes and the webserver replaces them)

Actually I'm not sure bumping version at each PR is the right thing to do.

In "normal" projects the maintainer bumps the version when he does a release. Between releases there is dev, alpha and beta versions.

If we want to encourage faster merges (aka optimistic merges) we need a mechanism to mark releases that the maintainer deems stable.

So in the current state of affairs, I believe it's better to *not* force bumping versions in each PR and let the maintainer do it manually when he thinks the module is stable enough.

By and large, Schematics has a lax approach to type casting. I've been wanting to make it less so, or at least make the behavior configurable. This has already been addressed w.r.t numbers; see my monologue and the resulting patch in #364.

In short, I ended up making `IntType` stricter across the board. A super-strict mode can be enabled by setting the `strict` flag on a field-by-field basis. Something like that should probably be implemented for `StringType` as well.

One thing I'd love to hear people's thoughts on is the relative importance of controlling type strictness at **data import time** as opposed to **field definition time**. Should we have both? If yes, how should the settings interact?

I'm not sure I'm following 100%, but it sounds like you have a couple different problems you want to solve, that you could probably solve in a different way now:

- **How to force modules to include a commit id.** The easiest way to do this is to make sure that whenever you add module `foo` to your `peru.yaml` file, you immediately run `peru reup foo`. That will give the new module a `rev` field, and then you can check it in. (We don't want to make it an error to leave out the `rev`, because some people prefer to always pull from master by default.)
- **How to see which modules can be updated, without actually updating them.** We should add a flag for this. Maybe `peru reup --dry-run`. But until then, the easiest thing to do is to just make sure your `peru.yaml` file is under version control. Then you can do a regular `peru reup` and look at all the changes, and if there are more than you want you just `git checkout -- peru.yaml` to undo the changes. You can then reup modules individually if that's needed.

python3 -c "print('\u65e5\u672c\u8a9e')"
```

It looks like `ANSI_X3.4-1968` is what Python falls back to when the system locale is bad. It's what I see on my own system if I run `LANG="foo" python3 -c "import sys; print(sys.stdout.encoding)"`. (Normally without messing with `LANG` I see `UTF-8`.) Is it possible you have a misconfigured `/etc/locale.conf` or something like that? Or was your system intentionally set up to be ASCII-only?

Ultimately I'm not sure what the Right Thing for peru to do is here. It would be easy enough to avoid printing our own unicode brackets when a terminal is in ASCII mode, but we'd still have to worry about unicode in git URLs, for example. Do you know if this is something that other Python tools explicitly handle? Maybe there's a hack we can use to tell Python to just emit UTF8 anyway?

> Actually, if directory contents should not be downloadable, it can be restricted on the webserver. 

Django assumes that the media directory is public by default. I think we would be setting projects up for failure if we contravene that assumption by saving potentially sensitive information to it. Everything else in the media directory is public (images etc). Invoices on the other hand should only be accessible to the customer that they are for, and administrators?

>Also, admin users might often need to check invoice for administrative/financial reasons, which usually happened on our projects. 

That is fine - but surely they should be using permission based views to do that?

>Finally, where would you suggest to store documents, out of the media directory?

Documents that need permissions should not be in the media directory because it is public. They should be served through permission-based views. If that includes PDFs etc then you would use `X-Accel-Redirect` etc to ensure that the web server can serve them securely.

One additional suggestion: If you're uncomfortable with the relatively large number of dependencies, just create a small lxc instance (approximately 350MB in size at the moment) and follow the aforementioned manual installation:
`lxc-create -n letsencrypt -t ubuntu`

In order to use the certificates on the host and allow for the automated tests, just add two bind mounts to your config as follows (the idea is that an external web server--either on the host or living in another container--will take care of this):
`lxc.mount.entry = /var/www/letsencrypt var/www/letsencrypt none bind 0 0`
`lxc.mount.entry = /etc/letsencrypt etc/letsencrypt none bind 0 0`

(Of course, you need to trigger synchronised renewals/web server service restarts from the outside of the _letsencrypt_ container, but it's pretty convenient...)

@Crazee, thanks for the suggestion. We don't have a way to verify people's assertions about what organizations operate servers, and we can't include information in the certificate that we can't verify. Letting people include unverified self-asserted information in the certificate itself is a vector for confusion for relying parties. Formally asserting which organization operates a service is a part of OV cert issuance rather than DV, and Let's Encrypt has decided to do only DV because we know how to fully automate it.

OK I get a solution after reading and testing https://github.com/python-visualization/folium/issues/135
on "How to use folium with iPython / Jupyter notebook".

```python
from IPython.display import HTML, display

def inline_map(m):
    if isinstance(m, folium.folium.Map):
        m._build_map()
        srcdoc = "data:text/html;base64," + m.HTML.encode('base64')
        embed = HTML('<iframe src="{}" '
                 'style="width: {}px; height: {}px; '
                 'border: none"></iframe>'.format(srcdoc, m.width, m.height))
    else:
        raise ValueError('{!r} is not a folium Map instance.')
    return embed
```

Using that inline_map function that encodes the src of the iframe in base64 allows correct filling colors for polygon markers.

So this solution is still usefull for present stable release 1.5
With 1.6, no more use of a inline_map since you just have to left the mapobject as the last line in the notebook and get a rich HTML display as expected. 
See https://github.com/python-visualization/folium/pull/190
The display of filled colors are correct.

fields = {}

You can put an "If" statement in front of any line and thus make it conditional:
```python
fields = {}
fields['base'] = marshmallow.Integer()

This is expected - at high regularisation rates full regularisation passes
are triggered repeatedly during an epoch, holding up all the threads until
the work is finished. I can probably optimize this a little more.

The result of this change should be that:
1. There should not be any performance implications with low regularisation
rates.
2. When regularisation rates are large, the algorithm will be slow but
numerically stable (as opposed to fast but producing nonsense).

This is a reasonable question. I don't recall it being posed before.
Suppose you do real modulus with a positive constant, then one would expect the quotient-remainder property:

      0 <= (X mod k) < k, 
      k*(X quot k) + (X mod k) = X,
      is_int(X quot k)

One thing you can do is to create new uninterpreted functions "mod" and "quot" that work on reals and instantiate these axioms every time you use "mod". This is what the linear arithmetic solver does
under the hood anyway for the integer versions of these functions.
It is not too likely we would add built-in support for this function given that it can be encoded and apparently rare to see a demand for.

>  how does versioning of those releases work. Also for major releases, is there a good way to include release notes with an overview of major changes?

@oruebel This corresponds to the latest release buildable of the `dev` branch and installable using `pip install pynwb -f https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/releases/tag/latest`, 

when a stable release (e,g vX.Y.Z) will have to be done:
* the release will be tagged, then CI will upload package with the proper naming convention (see #137)
* "release" ready packages will be uploaded to the latest release 
* the developer in charge of the release will then:
  * download the package from the "latest" staging area (it can be done with a one liner)
  * signed and uploaded to pypi.test using `twine` python cli
  * check that it looks good
  * signed and uploaded to pypi using `twine` python cli
  * then we will have instruction to also easily create the github release associated

from django.contrib import admin
from import_export import resources
from import_export.admin import ImportExportMixin

from .models import FrenchWord
from .models import ItalianWord

# import_export resources
class FrenchWordResource(resources.ModelResource):
    class Meta:
        model = FrenchWord

class ItalianWordResource(resources.ModelResource):
    class Meta:
        model = ItalianWord

# Integration with admin panel
class FrenchWordAdmin(ImportExportMixin, admin.ModelAdmin):
    resource_class = FrenchWordResource
    pass

class ItalianWordAdmin(ImportExportMixin, admin.ModelAdmin):
    resource_class = ItalianWordResource
    pass

admin.site.register(FrenchWord, FrenchWordAdmin)
admin.site.register(ItalianWord, ItalianWordAdmin)
```

The specific case you discuss is hard because angr currently doesn't support the raise library function or the kill syscall. If you wanted, you could write a short SimProcedure for the raise library function that either throws a `simuvex.s_errors.SimSegfaultError(self.state.scratch.bbl_addr, 'raise')` (this will throw the same error that STRICT_PAGE_ACCESS will throw), or mark the path as segfaulted in some other way convenient to your analysis.

Yes, mps-youtube supports mpris2 API! There is no proper documentation at the moment to learn how to get this working. So, I'll write whatever I know.

- You'll first need to remove any old libraries and install newer ones.
```bash
$ apt purge python3-gobject python3-dbus
$ apt install libgirepository1.0-dev libcairo2-dev
$ pip3 install PyGObject
$ pip3 install dbus-python
```
- Install [playerctl](https://github.com/acrisci/playerctl#installing) (easy to use, there are many others too) to control mpris supported players.

- Then run your `mpsyt` instance (re-run it if already running) and play this song (my favorite at the moment) in 2nd terminal.
```
$ mpsyt playurl https://youtube.com/watch?v=HQnC1UHBvWA
```

- Run this to list your running dbus instances.
```bash
$ playerctl --list-all
# This should display something like
# mps-youtube.instance24818
```

- Let's pause the current song.
```bash
$ playerctl --player=mps-youtube.instance24818 pause
```

- If the above works you're good to go!
```bash
$ playerctl --help
```

FYI mps-youtube sets the bus name as https://github.com/mps-youtube/mps-youtube/blob/9771d33d390dd6550f82bba16df4871d590071c3/mps_youtube/mpris.py#L36-L38

We have several different problems with this system which should always be dealt with differently.

1. Objects on disk are a subset of what is required by the Schema.
I think in this case correct behaviour is that we load the object and populate the missing data with default values.
 * How do we know that these values reproduce the behaviour we expected if the Job was constructed before this configurable was available?

2. Objects on disk are a superset of what is required by the Schema.
 * Currently the object is just loaded if the version is the same.
 * I think what we should be doing is taking the extra data from disk and making sure it's stored with the object for when it's modified and written to disk again.
 * Currently this information is destroyed as per @milliams observations.

3. **How do we know if it's safe to load a job if the Schema has changed?**

4. If we assume the Schema is always compatible with the information stored on disk then we should be explicitly allowed to load it for monitoring etc:
 * Is this extra information just extra configurables/options not used by this instance of the object?
 * Does this change effect a relatively cosmetic issue (e.g. parallel_submit) or does this change represent a bigger change such as inputfiles vs inputdata?

5. If we explicitly define that any change in the Schema are non-compatible should we lock the job and warn the user?
 * This now means users aren't be able to load a job that has been created in a newer version of Ganga when they downgrade. (This happens a LOT atm)
 * This also means newer versions of Ganga can't load older jobs just due to adding a new feature to the Schema. This means jobs have to be monitored with the version of Ganga that created them.


I apologise if these examples appear crude but they are questions that I think crop up if we don't have some way of knowing if an object has changed in a forwards/backwards incompatible way:


##### Bad example:
There is an external API change for some external service in handling new jobs, but all old jobs need to be correctly handled and updated through the monitoring system.

The old/new Jobs explicitly define their behaviour on some changed part of the external API, how can we tell that the job is compatible if the schema doesn't change? (e.g. something like where output is stored or protocols used for some communication)
If we load and mangle the job with the new API then we could potentially destroy the old job information with no way to revert, is this a good thing?

e.g:
1. myApp in Ganga v7r1 creates a job which requires a certain application configurable to know where data is. The default value corresponds to eos.
2. myApp in Ganga v7r2 no longer supports eos but the schema exposed to the user doesn't know this.
3. myApp from Ganga v7r1 is loaded with v7r2 and fails due to now being able to cope with eos storage.
4. Ganga v7r2 writes the job back to disk and Ganga v7r1 now has a problem loading the job correctly due to some minor schema change.

(I know the solution would be to store this information within the schema but what if we never expected to change this when constructing the Schema?)

##### Another problem:

If the schema is assumed to be compatible:

1. Ganga v7r1 creates a job
2. Ganga v7r2 loads the job and appends the schema and writes it to disk
3. Ganga v7r1 is now used by the user because they didn't/couldn't use v7r2. How does Ganga v7r1 know what the changed Schema is compatible?
4. Ganga v7r1 attempts to load a newer job from Ganga v7r2 and mangles the job as the new schema attribute changed how other objects in the schema are used.

If the schema change is assumed to be non compatible:

1. Ganga v7r1p0 creates a job
2. Ganga v7r1p1 modified the job definition to allow for the user to configure some behaviour.
3. Ganga v7r1p1 cannot load a job from Ganga v7r1p0 even though Ganga v7r1p0 is being deprecated and has a known bug.
4. After many cycles of this users don't know which version of Ganga is safe to use.

It's not the string comparisons that are slow, it's the fact that for every comparison it has to check against every item in the list. A container like a set will used binned hashes to do the search in approximately `ln(N)`-time. For 25000 items this approx 2500 times faster.

pyotp uses urllib to do the URI encoding. In python2, urllib doesn't have proper Unicode support; `quote()` expects bytes where it should expect a string. The solution is to either switch to Python 3, or to encode your unicode strings into byte strings before passing them over to `provisioning_uri()`.

I could make this behavior conditional on Python version in pyotp, but I would prefer not to.

The implementation in PyWavelets is via (downsampled) convolution with the wavelet coefficient filter banks.

The cython code called by the `dwt` python function is here:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/5fc0f769dfdd6d21c2d0b2fa4fa8cdb7e887bb9f/pywt/_extensions/_dwt.pyx#L22

That cython code calls C functions defined in `wt.c.template`
`https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/5fc0f769dfdd6d21c2d0b2fa4fa8cdb7e887bb9f/pywt/_extensions/c/wt.template.c#L273

which call the convolution routines from `convolution.c.template`
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/5fc0f769dfdd6d21c2d0b2fa4fa8cdb7e887bb9f/pywt/_extensions/c/convolution.template.c#L107

The convolution code is a bit complicated due to the variety of different edge handling modes implemented.

The wavelet coefficients themselves are defined here:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/5fc0f769dfdd6d21c2d0b2fa4fa8cdb7e887bb9f/pywt/_extensions/c/wavelets_coeffs.template.h

My understanding of the situation is the `pg_table_def` is the only place in Redshift that holds a complete column-level account of distkey, sortkey, and encoding configuration. SVV_TABLE_INFO and table_info.sql present the same partial view of this info (they both present a table-level view that tells whether any column encoding is set, what the first element of the sort key is, etc.).

`pg_table_def` is named as if it's a Postgres table, but it's Redshift-specific. It appears to be the only `pg_*` table that's Redshift-specific, and the only `pg_*` table that has this search_path restriction.

The PG dialect doesn't have this visibility problem because it doesn't have to access the `pg_table_def` table.

You can see that [AWS also needs to manipulate the search_path in their column encoding utility](https://github.com/awslabs/amazon-redshift-utils/blob/8d8b4f2dc2fd0b84f42f39cefc5ad3898856ec27/src/ColumnEncodingUtility/analyze-schema-compression.py#L171-L174), which I take as confirmation that search_path manipulation is the only approach here that's going to be comprehensive.

The `SET` made here to modify the search_path specifies `LOCAL` so that it only takes effect within that transaction and shouldn't affect the search_path of the session overall.

As for the placement of the Pact files, yes, the output directly should be configurable (the Mock Service takes an argument `--pact-dir` that will save them where specified)

I should mention that I have little knowledge of the tensorboard/tensorflow internals and I was not aware of the database roadmap but to introduce the idea I had in mind I will first explain the hack we currently use. 

## Current Practice 

We usually write command line apps with [fire](https://github.com/google/python-fire) to run a bit of grid search from the command line. We can give the run a name from the command line and from the command line we also set any hyperparams (which optimiser, learning rate, dropout chance, etc). Something like: 

```
$ tensoryo train --lr 0.001 --opt adam --dropout 20 --name 8-layers 
```

The command line app `tensoryo` will create a log folder over at `<logdir>/8-layers-lr=0.001-opt=adam-dropout=20-id=<hash>` and train a model. 

Every run gets a randomised hash such that we can identify different id for each run. 

Out current hack involves pushing the hyperparams into the log name and afterwards the grabbing any relevant data from the tflogs using the `EventAccumulator`. With a few loops and `pandas` we get what we want: a simple summary of all models where all the hyperparams are encoded in the logfolder name. It is a hack, but works very well for us. 

The main usecase for these hyperparams though, at least for us, is to generate a large table of all the runs we've ever done in order to determine if perhaps there is a pattern. I imagine that this is the main usecase for most people: to be able to query summary statistics.

## What Might Be Better 

We are really just interested in getting a table with consistent summaries for every run. It would be great if tensorboard had a table view for something like: 

| id  | dropout  | lr    | alg | acc_train  | acc_val  |
|-----|----------|-------|-----|------|------|
| 8-layers-1   | 20       | 0.001 | adam| 0.97 | 0.96 |
| 8-layers-2   | 20       | 0.001 | adam| 0.94 | 0.95 |
| 8-layers-3   | 30       | 0.001 | sgd | 0.98 | 0.94 |
| 8-layers-4   | 30       | 0.001 | sgd | 0.99 | 0.93 |

You should be very easily be able to add support for this by having a summary method for it. This might look something like:

```
tf.summary.attribute('id', name + str(uuid()))
tf.summary.attribute('dropout`, 20)
tf.summary.attribute('lr', 0.001)
tf.summary.attribute('alg', stddev)
tf.summary.attribute('acc_train', training_loss.eval())
tf.summary.attribute('acc_val', validation_loss.eval())
```

Getting hyperparameters in general seems like a very hard problem but I reason that developers would not mind to explicitly set these things. Currently, I am not aware of this feature. 

We're looking into perhaps making a custom ui for this either way but it feels like a rather general feature for tensorboard that would adres the hyperparam issue. Am very curious to hear from you folks if this sounds sensible.

> I can add an API for the non-blocking. I am not sure chip vendors would have interrupts for the erase done event. Your otPlatFlashStatus() looks like a polling function. Here we want some thing like callback?

An alternative would be to make it called something like `ThreadError otPlatFlashStatusWait(int timeout)`. To poll, you would call with a timeout of zero, which would return `kThreadError_None` if the erase cycle was finished or `kThreadError_Busy` if the erase was in progress. If you wanted the function to block, you would pass in a non-zero timeout value.

I avoided suggesting that because I didn't feel like it was worth the loss of clarity. Erasing flash pages tends to be a pretty heavy operation that doesn't (shouldn't?) occur very often. I'm happy to have it simply be a polling kind of thing.

A callback is overkill, IMHO.

Also, I would personally prefer `otPlatFlashErasePage()` to only erase a single page at a time. This forces the caller to be more thoughtful about what they are doing and it simplifies the platform implementation.

This commit fixes bug in `control` method, which makes using `control('LF')` have not result.

Also, it seems that `cut` command here is implemented incorrectly due to [ESC/POS doc](http://content.epson.de/fileadmin/content/files/RSD/downloads/escpos.pdf)

Here is following command code is used:

```python
# Paper Cutter
_CUT_PAPER = lambda m: GS + b'V' + m
PAPER_FULL_CUT = _CUT_PAPER(b'\x00')  # Full cut paper
PAPER_PART_CUT = _CUT_PAPER(b'\x01')  # Partial cut paper
```

But, as documentation says, there are two possible functions signatures for `cut`:

Function A: ```GS V m```
Function B: ```GS V m n```

Where ```m``` is:
- (Function A) m = 0, 1, 48, 49
- (Function B m = 65, 66

And ```n``` for function B is number of lines to feed.

So for **Function A**, possible commands:

```python
_CUT_PAPER = lambda m: GS + b'V' + m
PAPER_FULL_CUT = _CUT_PAPER(b'\x00\x30')  # Full cut paper
PAPER_PART_CUT = _CUT_PAPER(b'\x01\0x31')  # Partial cut paper
```

And for **Function B**, possible commands:

```python
_CUT_PAPER = lambda m, n=1: GS + b'V' + m + six.int2byte(n)
PAPER_FULL_CUT = _CUT_PAPER(b'\x41', n)  # Full cut paper
PAPER_PART_CUT = _CUT_PAPER(b'\x42', n)  # Partial cut paper
```

Also, may be it is a good idea to add number of lines to feed as parametr to cut?

I ran into the same issue and my solution so far has been the following: The example here does not include datetime for ticks but it hopefully is obvious how this would work with a matplotlib figure generated with datetime ticks. I generated the graph I wanted using matplotlib and then used plotly tools to convert it into a plotly graph: ``` import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import plotly.tools as tls mpl_fig = plt.figure() x = np.linspace(np.pi, 3*np.pi, 1000) sinx = np.sin(x) ax = mpl_fig.add_subplot(111) ax.plot(x, sinx) ax.set_title('A Sine Curve') ax.set_ylabel('test') plt.tight_layout() plotly_fig = tls.mpl_to_plotly(mpl_fig) ``` The only issue with this is that the `mpl_to_plotly()` will set layout defaults which results in the plot pane not fitting the graph dimensions. To overcome this I checked the layout that is send by a `viz.line()` plot and overwrote the standard "layout" generated by mpl_to_plotly by a custom version. ``` plotly_fig["layout"] = {u'showlegend': ['simulation'], u'yaxis': {u'title': 'sin(x)'}, u'margin': {u'r': 60, u'b': 60, u'l': 60, u't': 60}, u'xaxis': {u'title': 'Time'}, u'title': "%s for %s" % (name, operator_name[op])} ``` This results in better treatment of image scaling in the plot panes of visdom. For all of this to work I also just added a custom function into `__init__.py` to allow me sending a plot to the server which I realise needs to be improved but I share it for the sake of the example here. ``` def mpl_converted(self, converted, win=None, env=None, opts=None, update=None): return self._send({ 'data': converted["data"], 'win': win, 'eid': env, 'layout': converted["layout"] }) ``` Any suggestions welcome, happy to contribute to the project with a PR (after comments and improvements) if that is wanted.

Incorporating ``python-prompt-toolkit`` prompts into ``cmd2`` isn't possible without creating essentially a completely different tool.

``cmd2`` is based on ``cmd`` which in turn is based on the readline C library or an equivalent.  ``prompt-toolkit`` is (among other things) a replacement for readline.  So there is a fundamental incompatibility at a low level.

A fork of ``cmd2`` which removed the inheritance from ``cmd`` and the dependence on readline and replaced it with usage of ``prompt-toolkit`` would make for an interesting project.  If anyone wants to undertake that they have my blessing and I would probably be willing to help out.  I recommend it be called something like ``cmd-prompt``.  This theoretical project could potentially combine the quick, easy, and feature-rich aspects of ``cmd2`` with the power and visual richness of ``prompt-toolkit``.  But I don't have the time to work on such a project at the moment ...

I think that b0202c1 addresses the ODR violation, by defining two type_caster<tuple> instances, enabling based on whether "implicit.h" is included, as before.

As far as I can tell, it isn't feasible to do this with a default template parameter as @wjakob suggested, because type_caster<tuple> is itself already a partial template specialization, which doesn't accept allow template parameters.  It also can't be done via a common base class, because that wouldn't solve the issue: the derived type_caster<tuple> would still be ODR violating, since it is has a different parent in different translation units.  So the approach here: stick all the common class definitions in a macro, with two classes using the type_caster SFINAE parameter to enable one or the other based on whether implicit.h has been included.

I think the current behaviour is there for consistency between the way `TypeStore` is used in builtins and user-defined classes. In the UI we would be having two modes for visualizing the state of the raiden node and performing operations on them. We assume that most of the functions in the https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/raiden_service.py#L327 RaidenAPI class have been exposed along with lots of getter functions plus events that we need to visualize the current state of the user in raiden.

a. Standard Mode
1. In this mode we assume that all the channels have been opened, the deposits have been made and the user can see all this in the UI. 
2. The user simply does a transfer by selecting the asset address, amount, partner address and  does the transfer.
3. User can see how much amount he has transferred to which user in the past and what is the current state.
4. User can settle the channel or close the channel.

b. Advanced Mode
1. This is for the advanced users where users get opportunity to open channels, deposit amount an amount in the channels,  along with closing, settling the channels.
2. User should be able to register endpoint.

All these points are up for discussion and improvement.

Regarding list by @Eitot: * single/double quotes:
I didn't change anything yet but personally I'd stick to the following scheme, which adds an actual meaning to the type of quotes used * *double quotes* - (`"`) - are used for strings in a natural language, i.e. help output, warning/error messages, informational output, but also comments (as long as they do not simply use `#`). Basically all strings that are intended to be read by a human (or should be treated as human input) * *single quotes* - (`'`) - are used for all other strings, i.e. tag names, property names, regexes, etc. that are used programmatically. * lower_case_names/camelCaseNames:
This is very inconsistent in our code files. I think in the long run we should also try to follow PEP 8 recommendations, but I don't think it's important enough to warrant explicit renaming. * comments as sentences (first word capitalised and full-stop)/comments lower-cased without full-stop:
I honestly don't care. I always try to make comments as short and clear as possible. If "by accident" a complete sentence results I usually add punctuation and capitalization, otherwise I keep it lowercase and unpunctuated. * Usage of multiline comments or not:
As above - clarity is most important and I usually decide what is most suitable as I go. I don't think we should apply strict rules. @oberstet: Yes, I use separate branches / PRs for all changes that are a little more complex an might break something. In this way I can allow unit tests to run before merging and it ensures I committed everything correctly (e.g. once I forgot to add an .svg needed by a new unittest).
I also like that I can squash my PRs which keeps the history clean while allowing to split the PR itself in smaller logical parts that are easier to review (e.g. bugfix + some refactoring + unittest + update to history.md). However I wouldn't switch to "no direct push to master" since for trivial changes (e.g. simply updating History.md) I'd find it tedious to always create a branch / wait for tests to finish / etc. :+1: regarding integration of `flake8` into CI.

Well, it is complicated. Outside of docker,

```
run:
  script
```
is `/bin/bash temp_script_name` which is the most comm case.

```
run: args='{script}'
  content
```
will run `/bin/bash content` which passes content to command line.

`script` does not have a default interpreter, so 
```
script:
   content
```
execute `temp_file_with_content` directly, and

```
script: args='{script}'
  content
```
will execute `content` directly.

Now, if I can make this work also for docker, you should be able to do

```
script: args='script', docker_image='blah'
    java ...
```

The root question is how to execute a file with

```
 java -Xmx4g -jar  GenomeAnalysisTK.jar -h
```
In this case you will have to have an interpreter. If `/bin/bash` does not exist, you will have to use `sh:` or `#!/path/to/sh`. If you want to use `java` as interpreter, you will have to figure out the syntax to run `java whatever_option a_temp_file_with_the_script`.

for context, the system was designed for irregular, non-BSP control and data-flow computation patterns with light-weight tasks. Given these assumptions, load balancing is achieved asymptotically and with high probability. It's not guaranteed to be instantaneous. Heavier-weight GPU tasks and gang scheduling semantics are a better fit for more centralized scheduler for two reasons: (1) scheduling time is a smaller percentage of the task's duration, so the latency of centralized scheduling is easier tolerated, (2) centralized placement decisions always work with fresh cluster state, guaranteeing precise bin-packing gang scheduling expects.

You can use `cv2.VideoCapture` to read from a named pipe. If you create a named pipe and write the twitch stream to that pipe.
```
mkfifo /tmp/stream
streamlink twitch.tv/legema best -o /tmp/stream
```
Then the data will be streamed to /tmp/stream and you can read it from a separate process.

```python
import cv2
import numpy


def extract(path):
    cap = cv2.VideoCapture(path)
    fnum = 0

    try:
        while True:
            # Capture frame-by-frame
            ret, frame = cap.read()
            if ret:
                average_color_per_row = numpy.average(frame, axis=0)
                average_color = numpy.average(average_color_per_row, axis=0)
                print(average_color)
    finally:
        # When everything done, release the capture
        cap.release()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    extract("/tmp/stream")
```

That script will read from the pipe and print the average color per frame :)

It's always difficult answering issues when people don't fill out the template. Please do this next time, so it is clear to us and everyone else which Streamlink version you are using.

> Available streams: 496k (worst), 234p_alt, 234p, 896k, 360p_alt, 360p (best)

In order to select the "best" stream, all of the available streams first need to be sorted by their "weight". Since there are different kind of formats being returned from the master playlist (resolution based with an unknown bitrate and bitrate based with an unknown resolution), Streamlink somehow needs to convert them into a common format. That's being done here:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.11.0/src/streamlink/plugin/plugin.py#L51-L88

As you can see, the streams which are described by their bitrate ("k" suffix) get their weight from the bitrate divided by `BIT_RATE_WEIGHT_RATIO`, which is equal to `2.8`, and streams which are described by their resolution ("p" suffix) get their weight from the video height (plus frame rate, if available). The number `2.8` was defined by Chrippa 5 years ago and hasn't been changed since, see ddad859c9d6c5c791722536f8051222ba255d3fa. This formula is basically just an assumption of how big the resolution of a stream might be according to its bitrate, but it's not an ideal solution.

The stream_weight method need to get overridden in the plugin to fix this issue, or someone could make the `BIT_RATE_WEIGHT_RATIO` more dynamic, but for now, you can solve your issue by simply selecting the intended stream (896k) instead of "best"...

1. Get on the development branch (see the [installation documentation](http://pybel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#get-the-latest))
2. When you're loading your graph using something like `pybel.from_path` you'll need to set the `citation_clearing` keyword to `False`. See the docs on the [BELGraph class](http://pybel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/datamodel.html#pybel.BELGraph) for an explanation of the keyword arguments, then apply them in your preferred `pybel.io` function outlined in the [input/output documentation](http://pybel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/io.html)

I could change some of the logic in `pybel.parser.ControlParser` to allow for toggling between the standard mode where citations clear all annotations (and are generally better for identifying errors) and a strict mode that assumes all `UNSET` annotations are done properly, and does not automatically clear citations. However, in this scenario, I can imagine that BEL documents produced without great care would cause even more issues that we would unfortunately never be able to catch. 

Additionally, I don't think it would be so difficult to write a script that solves this issue programatically. It could find all groups of lines that don't begin with a `SET` or `UNSET` keyword, then reorder the following lines that do begin with `SET` before the next group that doesn't begin with those. Great question. We documented the comm messages at https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/master/jupyter-widgets-schema/messages.md (ipywigets 6 implements version 1 of the protocol). 

Your message about not knowing where the display message is from is probably from not having a parent header tied to your message. Your comm messages should have a parent header of the message that originally executed the code - that's how the display system knows that the widget should be displayed in that particular cell. In other words, your comm messages should be in response to an execute_request message. so that the frontend knows what cell's output it should be putting things in.

The "polling loop with short sleep" trick can be a really useful hack, but unfortunately doesn't play nicely with modern computers. The problem is that each time we poll, we have to wake up the CPU, usually to do nothing. Imagine a process that has 10 tasks blocked in `WaitForSingleObject` – now it's waking up 1000 times/second, which means your CPU never goes into energy saving mode, which means you laptop's battery life gets cut in two or something. That's why I was saying we should use a thread – it lets our process actually go to sleep until something happens. (Though by all means do a zero timeout call first before starting a thread, just to make sure there's actually something to wait for.)

    BOOLCONSTANT: like true or false, the value itself is side data, same for all the following
